If the new Cor proposal doesnГўВҖВҷt provide some dead-simple way to create accessors, then weГўВҖВҷll wind up with 42 different modules to provide that and we wonГўВҖВҷt be that much further along than when we started.  There are so many decisions to be made with Cor , many of which would be dead wrong if we move too quickly and we donГўВҖВҷt want to get this wrong. And the lack of multi-methods and the inability to introspect signatures means the BUILDARGS and BUILD pain will remain in some form (though BUILD isnt' too bad).  Thus, we really want to have some separation of slots, attributes, and constructors, but making an easy syntax for that is is a challenge. As the old line goes, ГўВҖВңmaking things hard is easy; making things easy is hard.ГўВҖВқ And making a solution that is easy and does the right thing and that all Perl developers will like is impossible. And there will be developers looking at the final solution and sayind ГўВҖВңnoГўВҖВқ because their one pet peeve wasnГўВҖВҷt respected.  As for our initial work with the syntax, the core Perl devs liked what they saw in the initial proposal, but itГўВҖВҷs one thing to say ГўВҖВңshiny!ГўВҖВң. ItГўВҖВҷs another thing to hammer that shiny onto something that already exists. There are a number of approaches which can be taken, but they require hard decisions. For now, Cor will be focusing on the syntax.
title: Moose "has" a Problem

body: A Brief History of Private Spaceflight  The Four Phases of Market Evolution  Spaceflight is Entering the Stability Phase  Starship Changes Everything  Costs  Disruption  What the Future Holds  New Industries  Mining in Space  Disruptive Uses?  Investing  Be Patient     A Brief History of Private Spaceflight  At the end of the 20th century, spaceflight was considered a ГўВҖВңnationalГўВҖВқ endeavor, limited to a few countries such as the United States, Russia, France, China, Japan, and so on. Companies like Boeing, Honeywell, Roscomos, Arianespace, and others provided space offerings that were attached to national programs, not private programs.  But at the dawn of the 21st century, things started to change. Jeff Bezos founded Blue Origin  in 2000. Elon Musk founded SpaceX  in 2002. Richard Branson founded Virgin Galactic  in 2004. Blue Origin was in stealth mode. Elon Musk was laughed at. Virgin Galactic was viewed as the toy of a billionaire playboy. Bezos, Musk, and Branson, serial entrepreneurs all, saw a future for spaceflight that others had not. They wanted to create new industries.  At that time, VCs werenГўВҖВҷt interested in space startups. Investment funds didnГўВҖВҷt carry them in their portfolios. By 2014, when Google acquired satellite company Skybox Imaging  , that changed. The era of private spaceflight was starting to mature.     The OTRAG Rocket, 1977    But just as many people arenГўВҖВҷt aware that the internet started long before the mid-90s, private spaceflight began long before this century.  Commercial satellite launches began in the 1960s, long before humanity landed on the moon. The first amateur satellite, OSCAR I, was launched in 1961  , a scant four years after Sputnik, and the first commercial satellite, Telstar 1, was launched in 1962  .  And then there was OTRAG. OTRAG was a private rocket company founded in 1975 by Lutz Kayser, a German entrepreneur. The intent was to build low-cost rockets an order of magnitude cheaper than existing rockets. Amazingly, Kayser succeeded, but the history is tragic  .   KayserГўВҖВҷs activities made the great powers nervous. The USSR and France were not interested in Germany achieving an indigenous long-range rocket activity. American rocket makers were not interested in having a low-cost competitor. A propaganda campaign began, alleging OTRAG was a cover for German and South African nuclear cruise missile development. Crude Soviet-source disinformation was eagerly picked up and given credibility by the American mainstream media. The government of the Congo, [where OTRAG was launching from], was pressured by the Russians to withdraw permission to use the site. OTRAG left the country in April 1979.   They relocated to Libya, but the Libyan government seized their rockets in an attempt to create their own rocket program. Fortunately, without KayserГўВҖВҷs experience, they failed, and the dream of private spaceflight failed with it.  If Kayser succeeded, we might have our moon base by now. We might be on Mars already. Instead, weГўВҖВҷre still waiting, decades later.  ThereГўВҖВҷs a decent Wikipedia page that sums up the history of private spaceflight  , but mostly, the nascent industry sputtered along in fits and starts, usually followed by bankruptcy.  It wasnГўВҖВҷt until the 21st century that the private rocket industry exploded.   [1]  Now, everything is changing, but whatГўВҖВҷs going to change? To understand that, we have to know a bit about the evolution of markets.  The Four Phases of Market Evolution  Note : much of the following is derived from the work of Simon Wardley  , but IГўВҖВҷve adjusted some of the terminology.  Markets (and new products within them) tend to evolve through four maturity phases. Each has unique characteristics and should be treated differently. This is boring for most, but itГўВҖВҷs important. These phases are:   Novelty Phase : A new market emerges. Unknown how/if consumers will respond.  Stability Phase : The new market is stabilizing, but not necessarily mature. Still experimenting with different approaches.  Commodity Phase : Robust competition amongst competitors. Less product differentiation due to market maturity.  Utility Phase : Market is mature, widespread, very stable, and margins are thin. New markets are built on top of these.    Note : the above descriptions are far too brief. For extended descriptions, I have a brief article which explains them in more detail, including examples .   For private spaceflight, the ГўВҖВңnovelty phaseГўВҖВқ was when entrepreneurs started to see potential, but itГўВҖВҷs very high-risk, high-reward. As first movers, if they succeed (most did not, of course), they can reap tremendous benefits. This is why Elon Musk regularly tops the lists of the worldГўВҖВҷs richest people.  As we move from novelty to stability to commodity to utility, we see risk gradually decreasing:     Novel markets are very high-risk.    Why does risk decrease? There are various factors, including customers becoming more comfortable with the market and having new ideas how they can utilize the market. But to succeed in this evolution, the products in that market must be reliable . Would you buy a cell phone that only has service 50% of the time? Probably not.  What this means is that as markets mature, their products become more stable:     Stable markets are very predictable.    Now compare this with air travel  :   In the industryГўВҖВҷs embryonic era, accidents were disturbingly numerous. Ironically, 1929, the year of ГўВҖВңThe Great Crash,ГўВҖВқ was also one of the most crash-ridden in aviation history, with 24 fatal accidents reported. In both 1928 and 1929, the overall accident rate was about one per every million miles flown. In todayГўВҖВҷs system, an accident rate of that magnitude would result in a nearly incomprehensible 7,000 fatal accidents each year.   By 2017, there were no recorded deaths of passengers in commercial passenger jets  . In 2019, a National Safety CouncilГўВҖВҷs recent odds-of-dying data  reports that you have a 1 in 107 chance of dying in a car crash. Airline passenger? ГўВҖВңToo few deaths in 2019 to calculate odds.ГўВҖВқ  But what about spaceships? Astronaut Chris Hadfield reports that in 1981, you had a 1 in 9 chance of dying in a space shuttle launch. By 1995, the odds of dying had already dropped to 1 in 38. Of course, those are per flight, not per lifetime, but if this trend continues, will we see ourselves in the bizarre position of seeing our odds of dying on a spaceflight lower than our odds of dying in a car crash?     Today, no astronauts have died since the 2003 Columbia space shuttle disaster  . (Note: the last cosmonauts to die in space were in 1971  ). Though in one bizarre incident, an astronaut almost drowned on a July, 2013 spacewalk  .  That doesnГўВҖВҷt mean spaceflight is suddenly safe, but itГўВҖВҷs far safer than it ever has been.  Spaceflight is Entering the Stability Phase  Long viewed as a novelty, spaceflight is entering the stability phase. ItГўВҖВҷs not there yet, but itГўВҖВҷs on the verge and SpaceX is leading the way. While the novelty phase offers higher rewards, its risk level is too high for all but the most informed (and wealthy) investor. However, when the stability phase is entered, the market has begun to solidify, the products are becoming trusted, more investors can understand the market, and the risk has dropped tremendously. Of course, the reward has dropped, too, but that seems a fair trade-off.     Advanced Blue Origin Lander Prototype.    However, particularly if weГўВҖВҷre viewing things through a capitalist lens, itГўВҖВҷs hard to argue that a market is ГўВҖВңstabilizingГўВҖВқ if thereГўВҖВҷs only one real supplier. Blue Origin, despite their recent suborbital tourist flights, is struggling . Due to poor performance, investors worry about Virgin Galactic  and the new lawsuit against Richard Branson and Virgin Galactic chairman Chamath Palihapitiya over allegedly dumping shares while covering up spaceship defects  is not going to help matters.  Roscomos, the state-owned Russian space company has long struggled financially and recently announced steep budget cuts  are not going to help. Private Russian spaceflight company Dauria is fighting bankruptcy  . French company Arianespace reported a 30% increase in 2021 revenue  but their lack of reusable rockets and their promised reusable ГўВҖВңPrometheusГўВҖВқ engine (to be first launched eight years from when IГўВҖВҷm writing) being delayed means theyГўВҖВҷre falling further and further behind SpaceX. Worse, the impending arrival of Starship means Arianespace is in serious trouble  .  So with them not going anywhere, what happens if SpaceX collapses? Elon Musk has already warned his employees about potential bankruptcy if it canГўВҖВҷt speed up production  (though he later admitted this was unlikely). But IГўВҖВҷve talked to plenty of people about the space industry and most of them are unaware of the numerous successful competitors out there.  Virgin One was spun off from Virgin Galactic and shortly after its first successful launch of seven satellites  announced that NASA put them on their short list of satellite launch providers  . This doesnГўВҖВҷt guarantee theyГўВҖВҷll be selected for launches, but the private sector is watching them. They canГўВҖВҷt launch very large satellites, but their launches are only $12 to $15 million per launch  . TheyГўВҖВҷre a force to watch.  Rocket Lab and Firefly are also looking very competitive  :   Rocket LabГўВҖВҷs small Electron rocket goes for about $7 million per launch, FireflyГўВҖВҷs medium-sized Alpha rocket goes for $15 million per launch, and SpaceXГўВҖВҷs Falcon 9 is advertised at $62 million per launch.   ThereГўВҖВҷs also UP Aerospace  , United Launch Alliance  , SpaceForest  (services starting in 2023), and many others starting development. Raphael Roettgen  , on the TerraWatch podcost  , estimated that there are around 200 launch startups around the world today.  The market is booming and stabilizing. Customers can now choose between a wide varieties of rockets with different payload sizes and price points, but thereГўВҖВҷs still an enormous amount of risk and it will be a couple of decades before we can approach the commodity stage.  Starship Changes Everything  A few years ago, a friend in Nottingham told me she wanted to spend the weekend in Ireland, so she bought a plane ticket. When she changed her mind, she threw away the ticket. It was only ГӮВЈ20. No longer the domain of the wealthy, air travel is for everyone and this is (eventually) coming to space.  Costs  For the space shuttle, the average cost per launch for the last decade was around one billion dollars  . For Starship, Musk expects launch prices of less than ten million within two to three years  . Eventually, he projects those prices could be less than a million dollars a launch.   [2]  In contrast, the ConcordeГўВҖВҷs inaugural 1973 flight from Paris to Rio de Janeiro  , roughly 5,700 miles (9173 km), operating at a cost per flight of up to $5.20 per mile  , could be looking at just shy of $30,000 for that flight. Adjusted for inflation, thatГўВҖВҷs almost $190,000 today. However, the $5.20 figure was for a 1972 estimate. In reality, the Concorde was subject to wild cost overruns, so a $190,000 flight cost is certainly low.   [3]  That Starship may not be significantly more expensive than a Concorde is mind-boggling. The fact that Starship might actually cheaper than planes for long-haul flights is even more mind-boggling  .  But wait, thereГўВҖВҷs more! The cost of building the Boeing 737  , one of their least expensive aircraft, is just over $100 million. At the high end, Starships could cost around $37 million each  , but an optimistic mass production rate could drop that price to $5 million per Starship. This is insane .  Disruption  The lower costs, however, have a knock-on effect for the entire industry. The media tends to think of the cost of the launch, but the insanely high launch costs ripple through the industry. Want to make a lunar lander out of stainless steel? Too bad. Titanium is about 35 times as expensive as stainless steel  , but itГўВҖВҷs weighs half as much. With costs per kilogram in the thousands  , every penny counts.       Launch Vehicle  Payload cost per kg      Vanguard  $1,000,000    Space Shuttle  $54,500    Electron  $19,039    Ariane 5G  $9,167    Long March 3B  $4,412    Proton  $4,320    Falcon 9  $2,720    Falcon Heavy  $1,400    Starship  $100      Yes, Starship costs can potentially cost an order of magnitude less per kilogram. What else does that mean?  It means missions can be run in parallel, not in series.  Think about a string of electric lights. If theyГўВҖВҷre run in series and one goes out, theyГўВҖВҷre all out. If theyГўВҖВҷre run in parallel and one goes out, the rest keep working and you can compensate for the missing bulb.  Missions in space today are long series of single points of failure once launched. Consider a simple (ha!) robotic rover to Mars. ItГўВҖВҷs been devilishly hard to land on Mars, so we have to:   Design the rover  Order the parts for the rover  Ensure all tolerances are perfect for the rover  Assemble the rover  Test, test, test, and retest the rover  Return to step 2 if the parts arenГўВҖВҷt up to snuff  Return to step 1 if the design isnГўВҖВҷt up to snuff  Do all of this in a near-sterile environment so we donГўВҖВҷt contaminate Mars  Move the rover to the rocket  Have a perfect launch  Wait six to nine months while the rocket heads to Mars  Enter the Martian atmosphere at just the perfect angle  Survive the descent to the surface  Scream in agony as the dust storm covers the solar panels and kills the mission   I skipped a few steps in there, but prior to launch, costs are astronomically high to due having to be perfect , absolutely perfect , because once you launch, any failure at any single point renders all of the previous work moot.  With Starship, you could build multiple rovers, with much less costly materials (weight no longer being the major consideration), lower tolerances for many of the components, and fly all of them to Mars on multiple launch for less than the cost of a single rover today. A couple of flights fail? Fine, you still have a fleet of rovers on Mars. And no, you canГўВҖВҷt reduce all of the tolerances, but by building a fleet, the individual costs can be lowered as you gain more experience in procurement and assembly.  Heck, the Perseverance rover to Mars will have a projected final mission cost of almost three billion dollars  . MuskГўВҖВҷs long-term goal is to make a passenger ticket to Mars as low as $100,000  .  Starship changes everything.  What the Future Holds  Just as the internet has proven to be a hugely disruptive force for industry and society, so will the new space industry. But like the internet, we donГўВҖВҷt know what that impact will be.     New Industries  As we improve our rocket technologies, many of the new industries are already on the horizon. Space tourism on rockets and even the ISS have already happened. But space hotels are being actively planned  :     Orbital Assembly Corporation space hotel concept.    Real-time satellite imaging is widely available, high-speed, low-latency internet  is starting, and Japanese company Astroscale  is already planning to remove space debris and provide on site inspection and maintenance of satellites. ( Dealing with the ever-increasing threat of space junk is a serious problem  ).  Mining in Space  Of course, no story would be complete without discussing mining. If you are at all interested in space, youГўВҖВҷve long heard that the worldГўВҖВҷs first trillionaires will be space miners. The early space mining companies are gone  because they, like OTRAG, were early adopters who adopted too early. They generally anticipated that the first space mining would involve mining extremely valuable ... water.  Yes, water. They were going to sell it to spacecraft using various means of water propulsion  (seriously, the idea is picking up steam). However, the industry they wanted to support wasnГўВҖВҷt ready to be born, so the space mining companies drowned (forgive me). But water is going to be a seriously valuable resource in space. I describe this briefly in one of my public presentations  (if the video doesnГўВҖВҷt start at the correct spot, jump to 50:13):      Side note: IГўВҖВҷm also a public speaker, so if you want to have me give a talk about this, let me know !   But what else might we mine? We are facing a near-term global lithium shortage, so that would be awesome. But where do we find it? Lithium dissolves easily in water and is found in salt flat brines like those of Chile, or pegamites , a type of rock that forms in water-rich magmas. ItГўВҖВҷs estimated that the moon may have the same relative amount of lithium as Earth, but without water, it wouldnГўВҖВҷt be concentrated enough to efficiently mine. Mars? Maybe, but weГўВҖВҷre not sure. Ceres has been speculated as a source of minable lithium, but we canГўВҖВҷt send our mining robots on a hunch. We need to go prospecting to find what we need and currently, thatГўВҖВҷs too expensiveГўВҖВ”until Starship is fully operational.  The same goes for Rhodium and the other platinum-group metals. Without a low-cost ability to to prospect space, we canГўВҖВҷt send out mining equipment, much less miners.  But letГўВҖВҷs say we find a near-Earth asteroid with the metals we want. The cost, per load, of those materials needs to be lower than the cost of building our prospectors, launching, searching, building our mining robots, launching them, mining the metals, refining the metals on site, and then returning them to Earth. As it stands now, we canГўВҖВҷt meet those costs and even with Starship, getting them back to Earth is difficult. We canГўВҖВҷt use parachutes. Simply crashing them on our planet will destroy much of it in the atmosphere and needlessly endanger lives, so they need to be brought back in a rocket. ItГўВҖВҷs simply not economically feasible.  More accurately, asteroid mining isnГўВҖВҷt economically feasible until we have an established industry in space . At which point, all of that changes because we can build, launch, and return in space. ThatГўВҖВҷs much less expensive and we can use those materials to build out space-industry in a more cost-effective manner.  Sadly, thatГўВҖВҷs decades away. But asteroid mining might still be viable.  The moon is regularly hit with asteroids and probably about 20% of those are metallic. Building stations on the moon and using the materials there might be worthwhile...and weГўВҖВҷre already looking at lunar stations. Thus, we can do asteroid mining, so long as weГўВҖВҷre doing it on the moon.  The south pole of the moon is a potential site for setting up our stations  . It appears to have abundant water and if we send out plenty of robotic prospectors, finding a location near silicon and platinum deposits would give us the ability to mine the materials to make solar panels to power our lunar outposts. But we wonГўВҖВҷt know until we start prospecting.  Disruptive Uses?  WeГўВҖВҷve already discussed Starlink  , a mega-constellation of satellites to bring high-speed, low-latency internet connections to the entire planet. There are considerable challenges involved, including efforts to reduce their visibility  and not ruin ground-based astronomy. The long-term goal is to have over 30,000 satellites operational, so effort is being put into ensuring defunct satellites de-orbit as quickly as possible to mitigate the problem with space debris  .  But itГўВҖВҷs possible that Musk may be planning to become a trillionaire by creating space-based solar power generating stations  . These generate nice, clean solar power in space and beam it back to Earth. Before you think this is just science fiction, China is already planning on doing this  , but unlike SpaceX, they donГўВҖВҷt have access to Starship. That being said, the the long-term benefits outweigh the costs of launching via ChinaГўВҖВҷs Long March 5  rocket. China is pushing forward.  MuskГўВҖВҷs path may be less clear because if successful, he threatens the fossil-fuel industry and they will do everything they can to stop him (unless he creates a partnership with them, which I doubt). They will be lobbying like made, citing environmental concerns of the launches (oh, the irony), trying to find regulatory means of stopping him, and pointing out to politicians all the jobs their districts stand to lose if these stations are operational. This seems strange, but weГўВҖВҷve already seen this with Tesla Motors. This will be OTRAG all over again: political and market forces working hand-in-hand to block innovation.  There will be other disruptive markets to emerge from this industry sometimes being referred to as NewSpace  , but like Black Swan events  , we canГўВҖВҷt predict them in advance, and they will have enormous impacts on markets.   Note : space elevators will not be the disruptive force  . Not only are they probably decades away, but an ascent to space will likely take weeks.   Investing  Like OTRAG, early space mining companies died because they were too soon. Early investors in this ГўВҖВңnoveltyГўВҖВқ stage of the market lost a lot of money. Now that weГўВҖВҷre entering the stability phase, investing presents less risk, though less reward.  Before NewSpace started entering the stability phase, there were few, if any, VCs or financial services company offering services. Today, there are many of them. Today, investment giant Morgan Stanley is even offering space investing  . However, space investing today is mainly in the hands of the wealthy. Sadly, this is probably a good thing because as if often pointed out by investors, unless you put in 10,000 hours or more in deep study of NewSpace, you wonГўВҖВҷt have the expertise to evaluate investments.  NewSpace canГўВҖВҷt do the early internet thing of ГўВҖВңthrow money at the wall and hope it sticks.ГўВҖВқ The amounts of money involved means you have to have an extremely high chance of a payoff. These companies arenГўВҖВҷt investing in new technologies so much as theyГўВҖВҷre investing in markets with solid business plans. This sets this industry apart from the early days of the tulipmania  Internet Bubble.  Of course, other companies are now getting involved. The highly-regarded International Space University  (ISU), has a MasterГўВҖВҷs Programs in Space Studies and offers a wide variety of professional courses in different aspects of the space industry.  Be Patient  WeГўВҖВҷre at the NewSpace equivalent of the internet in the mid-90s. ItГўВҖВҷs booming tremendously, but due to the extremely high costs and high risks, itГўВҖВҷs being approached in a healthier manner than the internet boom. However, we wonГўВҖВҷt see internet speeds of advancement. For much of the internet, it was a matter of building ISPs and connecting people and letting the industry blossom. For NewSpace, the entire industry needs to be boostrapped, including the core infrastructure.  ItГўВҖВҷs not going to impact us directly todayГўВҖВ“no one is buying a cheap flight to an orbital hotelГўВҖВ“but itГўВҖВҷs coming and despite the naysayers, itГўВҖВҷs not stopping. And even if SpaceX unexpectedly goes bankrupt (it wonГўВҖВҷt), it will still keep coming. I donГўВҖВҷt know if IГўВҖВҷll live long enough for a vacation in space, but my daughter will. So will your children. So will many of you who read this.  The long-awaited promise of a future in space is finally arriving.
title: Time to Invest in Space?

body: In 2018, the documentary Amazing Grace  featuring Aretha Franklin was first released. It was filmed in 1972. You might wonder why it took almost five decades for a documentary of the top-selling gospel R&B album of all time to be released. Sure, Franklin sued twice to prevent its release, but weГўВҖВҷll skip that. The real reason is hilarious.     Sydney Pollack  , now known as one of the most influential filmmakers in history, was approached by Warner Brothers to film FranklinГўВҖВҷs concert in a church. He agreed immediately upon hearing her name. The camera crew set up and by the second night, word of her electrifying performance had spread so quickly that even Mick Jagger was in the audience. This was a legendary event.  Warner brothers didnГўВҖВҷt want to wait five decades for the film to be released, but to understand why it took so long, we need to understand a bit about how a movie is made.  You know how you take 100 pictures and maybe one or two are any good? ThatГўВҖВҷs a documentary. You see maybe an hour or two of something amazing, but behind the scenes (pun intended!) are many more hours of footage that never leave the cutting room floor. Some of that footage is awful. Some may be amazing, but it doesnГўВҖВҷt fit the directorГўВҖВҷs vision. For whatever reason, you shoot tons of footage and use almost none of it. For Amazing Grace, twenty hours of footage became 87 minutes of film. In other words, less than 10% of the footage was used.  A film camera, as you know, records images, not sound. ThatГўВҖВҷs for the microphone. The film and the sound for the film are thus recorded separately.  Later, when putting together a rough cut of the film, the ГўВҖВңsync soundГўВҖВқ (the actual sounds recorded while filming) gets synced with the film. YouГўВҖВҷve probably seen films where the sound doesnГўВҖВҷt quite match the actorГўВҖВҷs lips. This is sometimes known as ГўВҖВңlip flapГўВҖВқ and itГўВҖВҷs annoying as hell, so you need to get it right. With twenty hours of film and twenty hours of sound recording, how do you get it right?  Typically, we use a clapperboard  , also known as a ГўВҖВңfilm slate.ГўВҖВқ There are various styles, but they usually include at least the date, the production, the scene and the take. But the one thing they always include is that long bar across the top. YouГўВҖВҷve all seen video of someone yelling ГўВҖВңlights, camera, action!ГўВҖВқ And then the second assistant camera  holding the slate slaps the top down with a sharp ГўВҖВңclack.ГўВҖВқ     A film clapperboard Source     In the rough cut of the film, youГўВҖВҷll see that clapperboard being clapped. In the audio recording, thereГўВҖВҷs a sharp spike when that clapperboard makes that noise. You can then use those to sync the film and the sound.  Sydney Pollack forgot to bring clapperboards to the church. After, they discovered they couldnГўВҖВҷt sync the sound to the film. They shot the worldГўВҖВҷs greatest gospel documentary as a silent film.  By June of that year, Aretha FranklinГўВҖВҷs album had sold millions. The movie would have been a slam dunk. Via Hollywood Reporter  :   The choir director from the Watts recordings was brought in to try to lip-read the reels, but after months of work, only about 150 minutes of footage had been matched with sound, none of it adding up to a complete, useable song.   It wasnГўВҖВҷt until 2008, using computers to synchronize the film and the sound, was the movie finally complete. Aretha Franklin sued (twice) to prevent the release of the film. After she passed, an agreement was reached with her estate to release the film. It grossed less than $6 million dollars, a far cry from what it could have been, but Metacritic rates it 94 out of 100  , or ГўВҖВңUniversal Acclaim.ГўВҖВқ  Of course, Aretha Franklin deserves nothing less.   If youГўВҖВҷre interested in the film industry, Castaldo Academy  has produced a short video explaining the clapperboardГўВҖВҷs use.
title: The Documentary That Took 46 Years to Release

body: Introduction  Forget Europe  Working Holidays  Teaching English  Volunteer Work  Au Pair  Studying Abroad  Joining the Military  French Foreign Legion  Foreign Service Exam  Fantasy versus Reality  Conclusion     Introduction  Constantly on RedditГўВҖВҷs IWantOut community  , or if theyГўВҖВҷre a bit clued in, in expat forums, I see variations of the following question (the following was a real post on the now-defunct ГўВҖВңYahoo AnswersГўВҖВқ forums):   I would mainly like to move to Germany but I am wanting to move to Europe and learn fashion design. I donГўВҖВҷt know what sort of job I can get over there being so young and not speaking another language. I donГўВҖВҷt feel at home in the USA and finally did in Europe and I have to go back ! what should I do?   I call these fairy tale posts.  To figure out how to move to another country, you have to take stock of what you skills you possess which might help to make this happen. HereГўВҖВҷs the typical profile of people making fairy tale posts:   No life experience  No work experience  No education  No language skills  No money   This does not bode well for your chances, but you might have the one skill necessary to pull this off: determination.  To start with, read Why youГўВҖВҷll say ГўВҖВңnoГўВҖВқ to living abroad . I consider this the foundation post of the expat posts because itГўВҖВҷs critical to understanding the process. You have to make sure that you can seize any opportunity which comes along (this doesnГўВҖВҷt mean you should take any opportunity).  The very first thing you need to do is apply for your passport. If you donГўВҖВҷt have a passport, youГўВҖВҷre not serious about moving abroad. If you canГўВҖВҷt be bothered to get a passport, you may as well stop reading.  The second thing you need to do is take stock of your life and stop doing anything which might land you in legal trouble. Of course, thatГўВҖВҷs a very personal decision, but if you wind up with a criminal record, your limited chances of living abroad are approaching zero. Maybe you donГўВҖВҷt think the government should outlaw drugs, but your personal beliefs are meaningless when another country sees you have a drug conviction. You need to decide whatГўВҖВҷs more important to you.  The third thing you need to do is set your life up so you can leave as soon as is feasible. Long-term leases or owning pets might be an obstacle. However, working towards a college degree is a benefit, even if you donГўВҖВҷt get to leave tomorrow. Always ask yourself, ГўВҖВңwhatГўВҖВҷs stopping me from leaving now?ГўВҖВқ As agonizing as this may sound, it also means considering your partner, if you have one. Would you leave a girlfriend/boyfriend for a chance to move to New Zealand? You have to understand your motivations here and you should be prepared for tough choices.  If you do have serious work skills, and by ГўВҖВңseriousГўВҖВқ I mean ГўВҖВңhighly skilled laborГўВҖВқ, IГўВҖВҷll later be sharing a ГўВҖВңHow to get a work permitГўВҖВқ series, but if youГўВҖВҷre young and unskilled, you probably donГўВҖВҷt have a chance at a work permit. So what do you have?  Forget Europe  I donГўВҖВҷt really mean that, but if you think ГўВҖВңwhere can I moveГўВҖВқ instead of ГўВҖВңI want to move to EuropeГўВҖВқ, youГўВҖВҷll have many more opportunities. The biggest mistake I see many would-be expatriates make is forgetting that the world is round. For example, Montevideo is a large, cosmopolitan city and Uruguay is relatively easy to emigrate to, basically requiring around $1,500 a month income and a lot of paperwork  . Can you find a remote job that pays more than $1,500 a month? Maybe you can get to Uruguay. However, one person who could have done this told me ГўВҖВңI donГўВҖВҷt want to live in the sticksГўВҖВқ (US slang for ГўВҖВңmiddle of nowhereГўВҖВқ). Montevideo is not ГўВҖВңthe sticksГўВҖВқ, but if you donГўВҖВҷt do your research, how would you know?  Working Holidays  For young people, you might want to consider working holiday schemes  . Australia and New Zealand offer them and while they arenГўВҖВҷt designed to allow you to permanently live there, once youГўВҖВҷre there, your options will open up tremendously.  Teaching English  Many young people complain that they donГўВҖВҷt want to teach English abroad  . Too bad. ItГўВҖВҷs one of the most popular ways that young, unskilled workers can move to another country. Many people report heading to countries with a strong demand for English teachers and finding work under the table, but this is very risky. You might not find work and, if you do, youГўВҖВҷre probably an illegal immigrant and risk deportation. Being deported from a country means you probably canГўВҖВҷt get back in for many years. In fact, some countries wonГўВҖВҷt let you in if they find out that youГўВҖВҷve been deported from another country.  Volunteer Work  Many people are surprised to find out that volunteer positions around the world often require you to pay for the privilege of being a volunteer. Sadly, this is not a scam. Many volunteer organizations are poor and simply canГўВҖВҷt afford to fly you to Paris, put you up in a flat and feed you while you work for two hours a day baby sitting someone. Do your research as some ГўВҖВңvolunteerГўВҖВқ organizations are scams, but if you find a reputable volunteer organization, you can at least have a short time in another country and gain valuable life and work skills. Plus, it looks great on your rГғВ©sumГғВ©/CV.  Au Pair  An au pair is basically a young person (usually a woman) who travels to a new country, stays with a host family and looks after the children for a time. ItГўВҖВҷs usually a short-stay trip and an au pair is not a nanny. Families do this to expose their children to other cultures and, sometimes, because they enjoy giving people from other countries an opportunity to see the world. Be careful, though. While itГўВҖВҷs rare, some au pairs find themselves horribly abused by their host families. Work through a reputable agency and be safe.  Studying Abroad  Studying abroad offers you not only the chance to live in another country, it allows you to earn a degree which makes staying abroad much easier. In fact, many countries give preferential immigration treatment to students who have graduated from that countryГўВҖВҷs universities.  Many foreign universities are eligible for US federal financial aid. Other countries, such as Germany and Norway, have free university programs which foreigners are eligible for. This probably means staying in the US for a couple of years, going to university and earning good enough grades to qualify for foreign universities. If you can do this, itГўВҖВҷs one of the best ways of getting out and staying out.  Joining the Military  This one is controversial, but yes, the military will often send you abroad. ItГўВҖВҷs one of the easiest ways of gaining life skills, work experience, seeing the world and saving money for college. Of course, you might have to kill the foreigners you meet. Your own values/political beliefs will have to be examined strongly before you consider this.  French Foreign Legion  Sorry ladies, this one is for the men only.  I used to run a very popular (now defunct) expat blog and every week, searches for ГўВҖВңFrench Foreign LegionГўВҖВқ showed up in the list of most popular searches on the site. I did quite a bit of reading about them and learned that many people think the Foreign Legion of today is the Foreign Legion they saw in black-and-white movies, or read about in Soldier of Fortune magazine. In particular, there are three myths I want to dispel about the modern Legion:   They no longer accept felons  The no longer grant French citizenship under new identity  Most applicants are rejected   To join them, you need to be a young man, in good health, and buy a plane ticket to France. And be willing to kill people.  HereГўВҖВҷs their web site  .  Foreign Service Exam  This is not as widely discussed and I should write more about it, but if you are interested in the US Foreign Service, the qualifications are:   U.S. citizens on the date they submit their registration package  At least 20 years old and no older than 59 years of age on the day you submit your registration  At least 21 years old and not yet 60 on the day you are appointed as a Foreign Service Officer   Note that neither foreign language skills nor a college degree are required. However, very few people pass their selection criteria. Prepare to do a lot of reading about being a foreign service officer  and accept that very few people pass the entrance exam. It takes a lot of study and discipline, but itГўВҖВҷs one of many strategies available to you.  Fantasy versus Reality  When people as me what itГўВҖВҷs like to be an expat, I often reply ГўВҖВңhow do you feel about immigrantsГўВҖВқ? ItГўВҖВҷs hard being an expat. DonГўВҖВҷt underestimate homesickness. Many people want to be expats so they can tell their friends what wonderful and exciting lives they have but their friends are thousands of miles away. And the expat life is not glamorous. The French have a saying, ГӮВ« metro, boulot, dodo ГӮВ». That means ГўВҖВңsubway, work, sleepГўВҖВқ. ItГўВҖВҷs a typical ГўВҖВңday in the lifeГўВҖВқ. Being an expat means that youГўВҖВҷll get up, go to work, go home, have dinner, and sleep. ThatГўВҖВҷs the majority of your existence at home and that will be the majority of your existence abroad. ItГўВҖВҷs the things outside of ГӮВ« metro, boulot, dodo ГӮВ» which make being an expat so rewarding, but those arenГўВҖВҷt the bulk of your existence.  ItГўВҖВҷs not a magical, fairy-tale world out there, but itГўВҖВҷs an incredible one. Immersing yourself in other cultures and understanding how other people see the world is one of the most wonderful things you can do in life.  Conclusion  If youГўВҖВҷre young and determined, you have multiple avenues to live abroad, either permanently or temporarily. I would strongly recommend that you pursue several at the same time, maximizing your flexibility. Living in another country is not a right, itГўВҖВҷs a privilege that you have to earn. If youГўВҖВҷre determined enough, you can do it.  And keep reading this blog. As I research more ways to get out, IГўВҖВҷll post them here, along with my personal experiences. IГўВҖВҷve helped others leave their countries and I want to help you, too.
title: Young Person's Guide to Moving Abroad

body: The Continuum of Everything  What Is Life?  Life as Biopresentation  Objections to Biopresentation  Why is rethinking life important?  Is Biology the ContinuumГўВҖВҷs End?     The Continuum of Everything  I recently wrote about searching for extraterrestrial life in our solar system . In that post, I put forward a novel way of defining life that I call biopresentation . So it was with some surprise when I listened to a recent podcast of Isaac Arthur entitled ГўВҖВңPost-Science CivilizationsГўВҖВқ and realized that part of what he was explaining reached right into the heart of my argument and even expanded it. HereГўВҖВҷs the YouTube video for it:     In this, he discusses what would happen if we ГўВҖВңrun out of science,ГўВҖВқ though there are several possible paths to that. As someone who loves chess, one of the things I appreciated was him pointing out that knowing all the rules of chess doesnГўВҖВҷt mean youГўВҖВҷre a master of it. So if we learn all the rules of physics, that doesnГўВҖВҷt mean we can instantly figure out what to do with them.  However, my big ГўВҖВңa ha!ГўВҖВқ moment was Isaac Arthur pointing out that chemistry is emergent from physics and biology is emergent from chemistry. For now, weГўВҖВҷll avoid the obvious, and unanswerable, question: ГўВҖВңwhat, if anything, is physics emergent from?ГўВҖВқ  Since chemistry emerges from physics, chemistry is physics. Since biology emerges from chemistry, biology is chemistry, but itГўВҖВҷs physics, too. Now for some, this seems trivial. After all, weГўВҖВҷve learned from astronauts in microgravity that our body needs gravity to function well. But it turns out that life might not only require quantum mechanics to lead to chemistry, but life might be actively requiring quantum mechanics to continue existing. ThereГўВҖВҷs an entire field called quantum biology  . WeГўВҖВҷve known for over a decade that photosynthesis might rely on ГўВҖВңquantum coherenceГўВҖВқ to generate energy  . Quantum neurophysics  is a new field, too. Special relativity may be critical to our functioning, not just to our existence.   [1]  Getting back to the main point, the question we should ask is, ГўВҖВңif biology is emergent from chemistry, where do we draw the line?ГўВҖВқ  We donГўВҖВҷt. ItГўВҖВҷs a continuum. Rather than refer you to a previous post I wrote, IГўВҖВҷll repeat myself.  What Is Life?  This must be prefaced with the ГўВҖВңlife as we know itГўВҖВқ caveat (and IГўВҖВҷll give examples later). And IГўВҖВҷll come right out and say whatГўВҖВҷs on my mind: most definitions of life are a steaming pile of shit.  One definition of life  , as taken from Merriam-Webster, is ГўВҖВңan organismic state characterized by capacity for metabolism, growth, reaction to stimuli, and reproduction.ГўВҖВқ   [2] But right off the bat we see a problem. Mules, for example, donГўВҖВҷt reproduce.   [3] Would you say theyГўВҖВҷre not alive? Of course not. Biologists get much more sophisticated than Merriam-Webster, but many of their definitions still boil down to a true/false value.  What about fire? It reacts to stimuli and grows  . We could even argue that it metabolizesГўВҖВ”what it consumes for energy to keep growingГўВҖВ”and reproduces by budding, but we donГўВҖВҷt think of it as ГўВҖВңalive.ГўВҖВқ  There are other properties of life, however. Homeostasis  is the bodyГўВҖВҷs ability to internally regulate itself and keep functioning. Does fire exhibit homeostasis? ItГўВҖВҷs hard to argue that it does. However, warm-blooded and cold-blooded animals have completely different approaches to homeostasis and homeostasis evolved over time  , so there are different levels of homeostasis. Maybe we can argue that humans have a homeostasis level of ten while fire has a homeostasis level of one? A piece of flint, however, would have a homeostasis level of zero.  But why would we argue that?  Life as Biopresentation  When I was working on ETL  systems to reduce the cost of Phase III clinical trials  , I often found myself working with data supplied by many pharmaceutical companies. Company A might refer to ГўВҖВңDr. Robert Smith, St. MaryГўВҖВҷs Hospital, Arlington, Virginia.ГўВҖВқ Company B might refer to ГўВҖВңDr. Bob Smith, St. MayГўВҖВҷs Hospital, Arlington, Virginia.ГўВҖВқ Are they the same person? ГўВҖВңSt. MayГўВҖВҷs HospitalГўВҖВқ might be a typo. ГўВҖВңBobГўВҖВқ is short for ГўВҖВңRobert.ГўВҖВқ Maybe they are the same person.  In reality, itГўВҖВҷs hard to definitively state ГўВҖВңyesГўВҖВқ or ГўВҖВңno.ГўВҖВқ Instead, I had access to tons of information about each researcher, including email addresses, phone numbers, birth dates, and so on. I would assign scores (how reliable) and weights (how important) to each piece of data and if two researchers crossed a certain threshold, they were probably the same researcher and we didnГўВҖВҷt have to spend valuable time and money trying to manually verify this.  This same approach should be taken to the definition of life. Instead of life being a binary ГўВҖВңyes or no,ГўВҖВқ we should describe its ГўВҖВңbiopresentation,ГўВҖВқ   [4] or ГўВҖВңhow present are the signs of life in a ГўВҖВҳthing.'ГўВҖВқ So for a virus:         Property  Score  Weight  Value      Metabolism  1  5  3    Homeostasis  0  4  0    Growth  0  2  0    Reproduction  3  4  12    Communication  1  5  5     In the above, a virus might have a biopresentation of 20. A human, however, would be radically different:         Property  Score  Weight  Value      Metabolism  5  5  25    Homeostasis  5  4  20    Growth  5  2  10    Reproduction  5  4  20    Communication  5  5  25     A human might have a biopresentation score of 100.   [5]  This would be particularly useful in describing abiogenesis. One hypothesis of abiogenesis is  :   Prebiotic atmosphere with ammonia, carbon dioxide and water vapor.  Lightning produces simple organic compounds that fall into solution in shallow water.  The compounds react further in a prebiotic broth, forming amino acids.  The amino acids link up with peptide bonds to form polypeptide chain proteins.  The proteins combine into more complex molecules that can replicate and metabolize simple substances.  Complex molecules and organic compounds form lipid membranes around themselves and start acting like living cells.   Traditionally, at step six is when we think ГўВҖВңlifeГўВҖВқ, but for biopresentation, we might starting thinking ГўВҖВңlifelikeГўВҖВқ at step five. Maybe even four. Instead of life being a fixed point, itГўВҖВҷs a gradual process. We donГўВҖВҷt point to a thing and say ГўВҖВңthis is aliveГўВҖВқ because that gets us back to the arguments of what life is or isnГўВҖВҷt. We simply describe its biopresentation.  This would be useful for biology today, too. Consider the endless arguments about whether or not viruses are live  . ItГўВҖВҷs often argued that viruses arenГўВҖВҷt alive because they canГўВҖВҷt metabolize outside of their host. That reduces metabolism to the primary (or sole) attribute of life.   [6] But metabolism is the global term for anabolism and catabolism  . Catobolism is just breaking complex stuff into simpler stuff while anabolism is using that simpler stuff to do more complex stuff. Fire catabolizes, but doesnГўВҖВҷt anabolize. Viruses do both. So maybe theyГўВҖВҷre alive? But some still reject that because, again, they canГўВҖВҷt do that without a suitable host.  But whatГўВҖВҷs a host? Obligate parasites such as the protozoan Plasmodium   [7] canГўВҖВҷt survive outside of their hosts. Or consider humans. If I drop you into the middle of interstellar space without a space suit, youГўВҖВҷll stop metabolizing, too. So the context in which we can biopresent is also a spectrum. Viruses are starting to look more alive.  But wait! ThereГўВҖВҷs more!  Most living beings have some ability to communicate with one another. Maybe all do. As it turns out, weГўВҖВҷve know for a few years that viruses can do this, too  . The human communication score might be at a ГўВҖВңten,ГўВҖВқ while viral communication might be a ГўВҖВңone,ГўВҖВқ but fireГўВҖВҷs communication is ГўВҖВңzero.ГўВҖВқ The weight of this factor is probably pretty high, so fireГўВҖВҷs biopresentation would be pretty low, but viruses could be easily crossing the threshold.  Pragmatist that I am, I want to skip the philosophical debates about whether or not something is alive and go with the pragmatic discussion of how closely something resembles life. However, we canГўВҖВҷt ignore the philosophical implications.  Objections to Biopresentation  At this point, I can see many biologists howling at the above. In a 2013 Wired article entitled ItГўВҖВҷs (Almost) Alive! Scientists Create a Near-Living Crystal  , the lede reads:   Three billion years after inanimate chemistry first became animate life, a newly synthesized laboratory compound is behaving in uncannily lifelike ways. The particles arenГўВҖВҷt truly alive ГўВҖВ” but theyГўВҖВҷre not far off, either.   The crystals created had metabolism and mobility, but couldnГўВҖВҷt reproduce. You can read the original study at The Royal Society website  .  For these crystals, one could argue that a), theyГўВҖВҷre not alive, or b), what their relative biopresentation level is. Some people would be uncomfortable describing crystals as alive, but if weГўВҖВҷre to search for life elsewhere in the universe, are we going to argue that life which doesnГўВҖВҷt share the same biochemical basis as our own isnГўВҖВҷt alive?  Or consider self-replicating Von Neumann machines  . By the biopresentation argument, they would likely score very high, but are machines ГўВҖВңaliveГўВҖВқ? Or apply biopresentation to The Gaia Hypothesis  . Is our planet alive? ThatГўВҖВҷs definitely a philosophical question, but in light of biopresentation, depending on what factors to include, we could make a strong argument that it is.  Why is rethinking life important?  If we go with a binary ГўВҖВңyes/noГўВҖВқ for life, it means that we have defined a limited, possibly incorrect, set of properties necessary to define life. Something not meeting those conditions might be disregarded. If, however, biopresentation is adopted, we can start defining ГўВҖВңlifelikeГўВҖВқ properties and perhaps not be so quick to dismiss something that is otherwise alive.   [8]  Is Biology the ContinuumГўВҖВҷs End?  If biology stems from chemistry stems from physics, is that the entire continuum? I donГўВҖВҷt think it is. I think itГўВҖВҷs pretty hard to describe viruses as being very sentient (the ability to feel and perceive things), but fish are. By the same token, it might be hard to describe fish as being particularly sapient (the ability to learn, self-reflect, and so on), though again, both sentience and sapience are probably continuums, as are most things complex.  So clearly biology can lead to sentience and sapience, but do they require biology? Possibly not. Consider the recent case of Blake Lemoine who contended that some of GoogleГўВҖВҷs software had become self-aware  . While I tend to agree with Google that Lemoine was wrong, I am not so sure how long I can hold that position in the face of greater AI advancements. Since this is just electrons flying around silicon, can we argue that sapience doesnГўВҖВҷt require biology? If so, the continuum could be across a tree or graph instead of a line. Or since humans built this, does that make the argument moot? I donГўВҖВҷt think this is possible to answer, but it raises other interesting questions.  If we have a complicated chemical reaction that biopresents as zero (ГўВҖВңpure chemistryГўВҖВқ), could sentience or sapience emerge from that? Could sapience emerge from pure physics? This gets into the metaphysical realm and we cannot answer that. It starts teasing out hints of a god or gods. While I am an atheist, many of my friends are not and these question approach the realm of the ineffable, a term frequently applied to God. I worry that I sound like the atheist equivalent of Reverend Teilhard de Chardin  , a Catholic priest who tried to unify science and religion.
title: Redefining Life as a Continuum

body: Introduction  The Drake Equation  (Not) Detecting Life  Assembly Theory  What If We Find Life?     Introduction  Few questions intrigue us as much as ГўВҖВңare we alone in the universe.ГўВҖВқ Like many, IГўВҖВҷm obsessed with the topic. So obsessed, in fact, that the National Library of Finland released an hour-long talk I gave on the subject.     IГўВҖВҷve also previously written on this topic and the above talk is an in-depth expansion of what IГўВҖВҷve written earlier, but hardly scratches the surface. But to understand a bit more about whether or not there is life elsewhere in the universe, letГўВҖВҷs look at some of the background.  The Drake Equation  The Drake Equation is a famous probabilistic equation created by Dr. Frank Drake in 1961. In this equation $N$ is the estimated number of intelligent civilizations we might be able to detect.  $$N = R_* \times f_p \times n_e \times f_l \times f_i \times f_c \times L$$   $R_*$ ГўВҖВ“ Average rate of annual star formation in the Milky Way galaxy  $f_p$ ГўВҖВ“ Fraction of those with planets  $n_e$ ГўВҖВ“ Fraction of those planets which can support life  $f_l$ ГўВҖВ“ Fraction of those which develop life  $f_i$ ГўВҖВ“ Fraction of those which develop intelligent life  $f_c$ ГўВҖВ“ Fraction of those generating detectable signals  $L$ ГўВҖВ“ Lifespan of those signals   The variables $R_{*}$ through $f_{c}$, when multiplied together, tell us how many intelligent civilizations ГўВҖВңmatureГўВҖВқ per year, to the point where they can be detected. Multiply that by $L$, the last term, and you have an estimate of the total number of intelligent civilizations we might detect at any one time.  At the time the equation was created, we didnГўВҖВҷt know any of those numbers, but Drake himself estimated that $N$ was around 10,000  . Today, we have been slowly knocking those numbers down, one by one. Of those we think we know the what the values are, we have:   $R_*$ ГўВҖВ“ Average rate of annual star formation in the Milky Way galaxy (6-7)  $f_p$ ГўВҖВ“ Fraction of those with planets (50% to 100%; probably closer to 100%)  $n_e$ ГўВҖВ“ Fraction of those planets which can support life (1?)   The last number, ГўВҖВңFraction of planets which can support lifeГўВҖВқ, is often reported as $1$, but we already know that many moons in our solar system might support life . So we could easily have multiple ГўВҖВңplanetsГўВҖВқ per system which could support life. We think there are between 100 to 400 billion stars in our galaxy  , so that could easily lead to over a trillion heavenly bodies which could support life in our galaxy alone!  We donГўВҖВҷt know how many stars are in our universe, but current estimate suggest there might be between $10^{22}$ to $10^{24}$ stars  . ThatГўВҖВҷs up to 1,000,000,000,000,000,000,000,000 stars!  So what about the number of planets which develop life, have intelligent life, and so on? We have no idea what those numbers are. However, that doesnГўВҖВҷt mean we know nothing about those numbers. We exist, Therefore, all of those numbers are greater than zero. The first, $f_l$ (fraction of planets which develop life) is what weГўВҖВҷll focus on here because if that number is too low, none of the others matter.  (Not) Detecting Life  You may have read in the news lately about a new method of detecting life on Mars: DNA sequencing at the picogram level to investigate life on Mars and Earth  . This is remarkable work, but to point out the obviousГўВҖВ”which the authors of the work are certainly aware ofГўВҖВ”what if the alien life doesnГўВҖВҷt have DNA?   [1] In a paper entitled Is There a Common Chemical Model for Life in the Universe?  , by Benner, Steven & Ricardo, Alonso and Carrigan, Matthew. (2005), point out that most ГўВҖВңuniversalГўВҖВқ probes for life target the ribosome, present in virtually all life on Earth. Again, what if the life doesnГўВҖВҷt have ribosomes? Using this as a tool to detect life wonГўВҖВҷt work and we know nothing about alien life (if it exists), so canГўВҖВҷt make strong statements about what that life is likely to be.  There might be another approach, though. Two Viking landers arrived on Mars in the 1970s and their labeled release experiment might have detected life . In short, in separate experiments conduction over 6,000 kilometers apart, the landers injected Martian regolith (the surface material on Mars) with a radioactive nutrient solution and something interacted with the solution and released the radioactive carbon-14. It looked an awful lot like metabolism. It might just have been a chemical reaction. We donГўВҖВҷt know.  WhatГўВҖВҷs interesting about the Viking Lander experiments is that they werenГўВҖВҷt searching for specific chemicals. They were looking for a biosignature indicative of life. Yes, we made assumptions about the ГўВҖВңnutrientГўВҖВқ solution, using seven molecules found in the Miller-Urey experiment  , but trying to have an agnostic approach to detecting life was promising. That brings us to Assembly Theory.  Assembly Theory  Detecting alien life is a tricky business because:   We donГўВҖВҷt know if it exists  If it does exist, we have no idea what itГўВҖВҷs composed of   Assembly Theory was first proposed by Professor Leroy Cronin  , the Regius Chair of Chemistry in the School of Chemistry at the University of Glasgow. Instead of just asking what a molecule is , he asked how it came to be. In other words, how was it assembled? HeГўВҖВҷs established the notion of a ГўВҖВңmolecular assembly indexГўВҖВқ (MA) which represents the minimum number of steps necessary to assemble a given molecule.   [2]  Consider the word ГўВҖВңgoing.ГўВҖВқ Imagine that each letter is an atom floating around which can freely attach to other atoms to form words. The MA of a single letter is zero because it already exists. However, when you add the letter ГўВҖВңoГўВҖВқ to the letter ГўВҖВңgГўВҖВқГўВҖВ”forming ГўВҖВңgoГўВҖВқГўВҖВ”you have an MA of 1, because one step has occurred. When you then add the letter ГўВҖВңiГўВҖВқГўВҖВ”forming ГўВҖВңgoiГўВҖВқГўВҖВ”you have an MA of 2, and so on. The word ГўВҖВңgoingГўВҖВқ therefore has an MA of 4. Of course, by the constraints weГўВҖВҷve listed, all other possible combinations, such as ГўВҖВңoinГўВҖВқ (MA of 2), ГўВҖВңinГўВҖВқ (MA of 1), and so on, are floating around in our primordial soup.  But consider the word ГўВҖВңhodgepodge.ГўВҖВқ By the time weГўВҖВҷve assembled ГўВҖВңhodgepГўВҖВқ, we have an MA of 5. You might think the word ГўВҖВңhodgepodgeГўВҖВқ has an MA of 9, but remember that substrings of ГўВҖВңhodgepodgeГўВҖВқ are floating around, too. One of those substrings is ГўВҖВңodgeГўВҖВқ and can attach to ГўВҖВңhodgepГўВҖВқ in a single step, giving us a final MA of 3, not 9 (ГўВҖВҳh' + ГўВҖВҳodge' + ГўВҖВҳp' + ГўВҖВҳodge').  Of course, chemistry is much harder than spelling. Molecules are 3D structures and individual atoms donГўВҖВҷt form bonds randomly with all other atoms. But you get the basic idea.  In the paper, Identifying molecules as biosignatures with assembly theory and mass spectrometry  , (Marshall, S.M., Mathis, C., Carrick, E. et al.), Professor Cronin and his colleagues took the time to:   calculate the complexity of several million molecules and validate that their complexity can be experimentally determined by mass spectrometry.   Their findings?   These results demonstrate that we can identify the living systems by looking for mixtures with an MA greater than a certain threshold. In the case of the analysis presented here it appears that only living samples produced MA measurement above ~15.   In short, molecules with an MA above 15 were invariably produced by life, while molecules below that might be produced by both abiotic or biotic processes. If these results hold, we might have a ГўВҖВңuniversalГўВҖВқ method of detecting life, with the caveat that if thereГўВҖВҷs life that only generates molecules with lower MA numbers, weГўВҖВҷll still not detect it. However, this approach seems rather promising.  To give some context, sometimes youГўВҖВҷll read breathless news articles proclaiming that weГўВҖВҷve found ГўВҖВңcomplexГўВҖВқ organic molecules in space. For context, organic molecules are just molecules with carbon-hydrogen or carbon-carbon bonds TheyГўВҖВҷre all over the place because carbon is one of the easiest molecules to form bonds with. One of those ГўВҖВңcomplexГўВҖВқ organic molecules found in space is ethyl formate. It has an MA of 3. The molecule n-propyl cyanide has also been seen in space. It also has an MA of 3.  In fact, most complex organic molecules in space have rather low MA numbers and itГўВҖВҷs not hard to imagine abiotic processes producing them. On the other hand, we may have detected tryptophan around the IC348 star system  , about 1,000 light years away. Tryptophan has an MA of 11.  (If youГўВҖВҷd like to check these numbers, I wrote a script to do that for you  , but it requires the Molecular Assembly REST API  . That doesnГўВҖВҷt always seem to return results. In correspondence with Dr. Cronin, he confirms theyГўВҖВҷre working on a new version)  Throughout all of the above, keep in mind that Assembly Theory, like all new scientific endeavors, has its detractors.  What If We Find Life?  Three things. Given that there are possibly a trillion or more candidate places for life to come into being in just our galaxy, given that we know $f_l$ is greater than zero, and given that weГўВҖВҷre seeing more and more complex chemistry in our own solar system which would be easy to explain biologically (were we to know the biology is there), IГўВҖВҷm gradually leaning to the idea that we are probably not alone in the universe. Of course, thatГўВҖВҷs a hunch, not science.  In the fifth century BCE, the Greek philosopher-scientist Anaxagorus speculated that there was life on the moon  . Many have speculated about this throughout history and certainly in pre-history. That we have so many religions asserting non-human intelligences with whom we can communicate is part of this longing of ours to know that weГўВҖВҷre not alone in the universe.  Finding life, but not intelligent life, will not be enough to satisfy us, but it will be an amazing step forwards in filling in the last of the variables in the Drake Equation. Further, if we find life in our own solar system, but it has a different origin from our own, itГўВҖВҷs virtually impossible that the only two abiogenesis events in the universe happened in the same solar system. The universe would be teeming with life.  For those wondering about abiogenesis (the origin of life from non-life), hereГўВҖВҷs a video by Sabine Hossenfelder describing our current thinking on the topic.
title: Searching for Alien Life

body: I wrote this a while ago, exploring first-person, present-tense, non-linear story telling. That wasnГўВҖВҷt the intent, but when the story came to me, it was there and it wouldnГўВҖВҷt go away. I donГўВҖВҷt say itГўВҖВҷs a great piece, but IГўВҖВҷm proud of it. I also find that first-person, present-tense makes it easier for me to avoid filter words  . I have no idea why. I have a chapter from my novel thatГўВҖВҷs written in first-person, present tense, and it still gives me chills to read it. Narcissism is fun!  ItГўВҖВҷs worth noting that the main character is based on a friend. We worked together and he told me what it was like growing up black, in a gang, in Portland, Oregon. IГўВҖВҷve taken a few liberties, but his voice rings strongly in my mind and I think IГўВҖВҷve been faithful to it, though IГўВҖВҷve toned it down. A white author writing about black experience cannot use the n-word.  The " now " and " then " are recent additions by me because some early readers complained it was too difficult to follow the non-linear story line. However, I canГўВҖВҷt simply re-arrange the story in a linear fashion because that destroys it. Hence, my added cues for the reader. If they donГўВҖВҷt help or distract, let me know in the comments. In fact, IГўВҖВҷd be grateful for any feedback you care to leave. I want to be a better writer.  Also, I know itГўВҖВҷs the US Marshals who handle the witness protection program. I wrote ГўВҖВңFBIГўВҖВқ because I wasnГўВҖВҷt sure most people would know who the US Marshals are.  The Names We Never Say  Now.  ГўВҖВңParents died in a house fire when I was a kid, so no, I donГўВҖВҷt have no childhood photos.ГўВҖВқ  That shuts ГўВҖВҳem up. It always does. I roll up my left sleeve. A mess of white scar tissue on black skin.  ГўВҖВңThatГўВҖВҷs my childhood photo.ГўВҖВқ  I got that from a night drinking and messing with a barbecue, but I didnГўВҖВҷt tell them that. Heck, I shouldnГўВҖВҷt even have shown them the scar, but lying always made me nervous and I felt like I had to back it up. Old, stupid habits die hard.  ГўВҖВңJames, IГўВҖВҷm so sorry.ГўВҖВқ FrankГўВҖВҷs squeezeГўВҖВ”donГўВҖВҷt even remember her name and thereГўВҖВҷs no point because sheГўВҖВҷll be gone next weekГўВҖВ”goes beet red with embarrassment. I feel sorry for her. FrankГўВҖВҷs my best friend now, but heГўВҖВҷs a bit of a bastard about women. It was the only thing we argued about. IГўВҖВҷve found God now. I think. Not sure if heГўВҖВҷs found me.  ГўВҖВңNo worries, girl. You didnГўВҖВҷt know. Gonna get another beer and if you two ainГўВҖВҷt drinkin' with me, IГўВҖВҷm gonna be mighty offended.ГўВҖВқ  Middle of a Saturday afternoon and FrankГўВҖВҷs here to watch the game with me. My wife and daughter are out on a mother/daughter field trip and I have a day off to watch LeBron destroy Miami. ItГўВҖВҷs a hot summer day and a few Coronas with lime go down a treat. IГўВҖВҷm in the kitchen, reaching in the refrigerator when I hear the clang of the mail slot in the front door.  I walk back into the living room and thereГўВҖВҷs Frank, reading an envelope.  ГўВҖВңWhoГўВҖВҷs Jamal?ГўВҖВқ  With that innocent question, my life is over for the third time.   Then.  You have no idea what itГўВҖВҷs like growing up near Martin Luther King Avenue in Portland, Oregon. I had the luxury of traveling a lot before I got busted the second time and every place that has an ГўВҖВңMLKГўВҖВқ Boulevard/Avenue/PromenadeГўВҖВ”nothing ever as pedestrian as ГўВҖВңStreetГўВҖВқГўВҖВ”is always through a poor black neighborhood and thatГўВҖВҷs where I grew up. ThatГўВҖВҷs where I dealt. ThatГўВҖВҷs where I joined a gang. ThatГўВҖВҷs where I lost my virginity and, as a result, acquired my first scar. A stab in the gut courtesy of her boyfriend.  IГўВҖВҷm not stupid. I didnГўВҖВҷt brag to nobody, but one night hanging out, there he was. His name was Aaron and he walked up, smiled, and said ГўВҖВңГўВҖВҳSup, Jamal?ГўВҖВқ  I just nodded and he slid a knife in my gut like it was the most natural thing in the world. To him, I suppose it was. People were screaming and Aaron just walked away.  I told the cops I didnГўВҖВҷt know who it was. You settle your own shit. Course they sent a black cop round. Like IГўВҖВҷm gonna trust one of them. I went looking for Aaron later. I thought I sort of deserved what I got, but you gotta save face, right? Everybody said Aaron disappeared. No idea where he went off to.   Then.  I didnГўВҖВҷt plan to grow up dealing; I wanted to go to college and study literature. Momma didnГўВҖВҷt have much, but she loved books. Naturally, I loved books too, but lettin' on that I huddled under the blanket, switching from Heinlein to Faulkner to ГўВҖВңPoets of the Nuyorican CafГғВ©ГўВҖВқ made me a joke. So publicly, I read biographies of Malcom X, Nelson Mandela, or anyone else that wasnГўВҖВҷt going to piss off my friends too much, though I liked them, too.  But nobody hired boys named ГўВҖВңJamal.ГўВҖВқ At least not good jobs. At least not in the whitest major city in the good ol' US of A, love her or leave her. Which is how I got busted the first time, dealing while working at a car wash. I wasnГўВҖВҷt stupid and IГўВҖВҷve got a feel for people, but itГўВҖВҷs not perfect. The undercovers dropped a name and asked if they could score. Later they lied their lilly-white asses off in court. There was no entrapment; I offered it to them. Guilty.  But I never grassed on no one. You donГўВҖВҷt get two years for a first offense, but with a name like ГўВҖВңJamalГўВҖВқ and refusing to cooperate, I had a DA in a bad mood. Got out six months early for good behavior, hit the streets and life was over.  I tried to go straight. I really did. But when you have to explain time in prison, you donГўВҖВҷt get no real jobs, which is why I felt real lucky to have Christine. She was AaronГўВҖВҷs ex, the one I slept with. He left her a broken nose as a parting gift, but she was lucky and it healed sort of upturned. It was cute, but I hated Aaron. You donГўВҖВҷt hit women. No way.  Christine was a receptionist at a car dealership and got me a job as a salesman. You piss clean in a cup and talk like nobodyГўВҖВҷs business and they donГўВҖВҷt care about your past. They just want to know if you can make them money.  Christine was special. For the first time in my life, I felt like things were going good. I didnГўВҖВҷt feel too comfortable round her mom, though. She didnГўВҖВҷt care about my pastГўВҖВ”too many good boys get caught up in bad thingsГўВҖВ”but she creeped me out. I was sitting there, sippin' iced tea, waiting for Christine and talking to ChristineГўВҖВҷs mom, Gisele. ГўВҖВңGee-seh-luh.ГўВҖВқ What sort of name is that?  You didnГўВҖВҷt lie to Gisele. I learned that early on. I never felt good about lyin'. I could lie when I had to, but not with Gisele. She was a human fuckin' lie detector. I once told her I sold 18 cars last month. I just wanted her to know I could take care of Christine. ThatГўВҖВҷs exactly what I was thinking. Gisele just looked at me and said ГўВҖВңI know youГўВҖВҷll take care of Christine, but you didnГўВҖВҷt sell no 18 cars last month, Jamal.ГўВҖВқ  She could have found out how many cars I sold, but how the hell did she know what I was thinking? I never lied to her again.  So there we were, drinking iced tea, and I said Clinton was going to crush Trump in the election. Gisele looked sad and said, ГўВҖВңno, sheГўВҖВҷs not.ГўВҖВқ  And just like that, I knew Clinton would lose. Gisele did that to you.  When Christine got home that night from college, she stepped into the living room and plunked herself down on the old, ratty sofa and curled up next to me. I wrapped my arms around Christine and knew with dead certainty that I loved her. Probably the only reason Gisele let me in the house. She knew.  And then she didnГўВҖВҷt let me in no more. I wasnГўВҖВҷt a great salesman, but the other guys knew my past and kept asking if I could hook ГўВҖВҳem up. ThatГўВҖВҷs how I got back in. I wanted to make money for Christine and here it was. Just one look at her big, brown eyes and IГўВҖВҷd have given her the world if I could. So I lied and said I was making good commission on my cars, when really I was hooking up the other salesmen. And then I started hooking up more than other salesman. ThatГўВҖВҷs how it is.   Now.  ГўВҖВңJamal? HeГўВҖВҷs my cousin in San Francisco. DonГўВҖВҷt know why IГўВҖВҷd be getting mail from him.ГўВҖВқ  Frank hands me the envelope and I look at the address. ГўВҖВңJamal, c/o James Hillman.ГўВҖВқ IГўВҖВҷm still Jamal inside, but Frank and everybody else knows IГўВҖВҷm James. Except for my wife, Angela, nГғВ©e Christine. Even our daughter, Sarah, donГўВҖВҷt know our real names. I hated lying to her about it, but I love her as much as Christine. No way in hell IГўВҖВҷm going to give her a gun to take her parents away from her.  I put the envelope aside and Frank forgets about it. Every nerve in my body is screaming, but I wander back to the TV, acting like nothing was wrong. I still donГўВҖВҷt know who won that game.   Then.  New apartment. New king size bed. Mind wandering.  ГўВҖВңWhat sort of name is Gisele? I never heard no name like that.ГўВҖВқ  Christine was lying in my arms, our bodies still covered with sweat, and whispered to me, ГўВҖВңher nameГўВҖВҷs not Gisele.ГўВҖВқ  ГўВҖВңWhat the hell does that mean?ГўВҖВқ  ГўВҖВңShe has this thing about names and wonГўВҖВҷt tell anyone her real one. Says it gives people power over you. And stop talking that way, youГўВҖВҷre better than that.ГўВҖВқ  ГўВҖВңSo whatГўВҖВҷs her name?ГўВҖВқ  Christine sat up and looked down on me.  ГўВҖВңNo, Jamal,ГўВҖВқ she said. ГўВҖВңShe maybe has weird ideas, but sheГўВҖВҷs my mother. I wonГўВҖВҷt dishonor her.ГўВҖВқ  Christine was sort of a Christian and honoring her mother and father (God rest his soul) was important to her. I say ГўВҖВңsort of a ChristianГўВҖВқ because she knew I was making more money than selling cars could provide, but she never asked. I liked to think itГўВҖВҷs because she loved me, but I never asked, either.  I sat up and wrapped my arms around Christine. I knew not to press about GiseleГўВҖВҷs name and hugged Christine tightly, but this bothered me. Everything about Gisele gave me the willies and at this point in my life, anything I didnГўВҖВҷt understand was a threat. Except I didnГўВҖВҷt understand Gisele and she didnГўВҖВҷt feel like no threat.   Then.  A couple of years before that, I got some really bad good news. I was a small-time dealer, now selling cars and coke and my supplier invited me to dinner on a night Christine was in school. I went round to the restaurant, but my supplier wasnГўВҖВҷt there. Lamonte was. Lamonte was my supplier when I went to prison. I never dropped his name, but maybe he didnГўВҖВҷt know that. But when he saw me, he smiled, and waved me over. ГўВҖВңSit down, Jamal. ItГўВҖВҷs time to talk about your future.ГўВҖВқ  I looked round, but no one in the restaurant said ГўВҖВңcopГўВҖВқ to me. Not even Lamonte. A waiter walked up and smiled, so I sat. I ordered a beer, took a menu, and waited for the bad news.  ГўВҖВңI hear you play straight. And I know you do. You could have sent me up, but you went to jail rather than drop my name.ГўВҖВқ  ГўВҖВңPrison. Jail may be shorter, but at least they got shit to do in prison.ГўВҖВқ  Lamonte nodded; he knew what I meant. You donГўВҖВҷt, but he does. In jail you sit on your ass and play cards, waiting a few months to get out. YouГўВҖВҷre bored stiff. In prison, youГўВҖВҷre there for a long time, so they have weight rooms, college classes, libraries, you name it. Jail doesnГўВҖВҷt have shit, but in prison, they give you a chance to better yourself. Maybe youГўВҖВҷll come out with a degree. Or maybe itГўВҖВҷs a crime finishing school, with professors who were stupid enough to get caught.  The waiter came by and I ordered. When he left, Lamonte leaned forward and said ГўВҖВңYou had my back when you didnГўВҖВҷt have to. Now I got yours. You say the word and you donГўВҖВҷt have to sell no more god-damned cars. IГўВҖВҷm doing good and I want people I can trust. I trust you.ГўВҖВқ  I sat back, scared as hell. Christine looked the other way about the extra cash I was earning, but thatГўВҖВҷs only because I had a job. She was the one good thing in my life and I didnГўВҖВҷt want to fuck it up. ThatГўВҖВҷs what I told Lamonte.  ГўВҖВңYouГўВҖВҷre a good man, Jamal. I appreciate what a good woman can do for a man, but what can you do for her? YouГўВҖВҷre a felon. You know youГўВҖВҷre never going to get a real job. Oh, you might be a sales manager some day, earn whatever small bit of green the man wants to toss your way, but you really want to take care of her? I can offer you that. ItГўВҖВҷs a real job. I start you at five grand a month and you run some dealers. I wonГўВҖВҷt lie to you. I ainГўВҖВҷt promisin' nothin' badГўВҖВҷll ever happen, but if it does, we got each otherГўВҖВҷs backs. And you treat us right, we treat you right. You can keep Christine in college and have nice things.ГўВҖВқ  And thatГўВҖВҷs how life ended the second time, but at least it paid well.   Now.  ItГўВҖВҷs two days before I open that envelope. I know itГўВҖВҷs stupid, but IГўВҖВҷm scared. I donГўВҖВҷt know what the hell to do. ChristineГўВҖВ”excuse meГўВҖВ”Angela probably knows something is up but I tell her itГўВҖВҷs just work. With the economy the way it is, she accepts that. I hate to lie to her, but IГўВҖВҷm really thinking about Sarah. When she was born, she brought joy to our lives like we had never experienced. Oh, we were as happy as our circumstances could allow, but this felt like it was for real this time. Past sins were absolved and beautiful Sarah laughed and cried and hiccoughed and I even started going to church. My life was almost complete. Even changing diapers had made me smile.  Sarah had never known any life but what we had. I didnГўВҖВҷt want to take her out of school, but thereГўВҖВҷs an envelope in my hand. Hell, I like going to PTA meetings. I want Sarah to have everything I didnГўВҖВҷt. And so did Angela. We have the life we always wanted. It isnГўВҖВҷt perfect, but itГўВҖВҷs stable. And always with that tinge of fear. Now I know I should call the FBI and I know they will take away our life and give us a new one. Sarah will cry for her friends in school and I have no idea what to say to her.  I donГўВҖВҷt know what to expect when I open that envelope, but I certainly donГўВҖВҷt expect what I find.  I unfold the paper and read. Or at least I thought I would. The paper is blank. I turn it over, but nothing is there. Paranoid, I look in the envelope for weird powder or whatever the hell anthrax looks like, but I see nothing. I sniff the air. Nothing. I sit. I wait.  I have tears running down my face when I notice the writing on the back of the envelope.  ГўВҖВңGisele.ГўВҖВқ  And then the mail slot clanged. There was another envelope on the floor.   Then.  Life working for Lamonte was good. Lamonte was careful and if a dealer started using, they were out. You couldnГўВҖВҷt trust them. I was one of several ГўВҖВңaccount managersГўВҖВқ and we were a tight group. We knew LamonteГўВҖВҷs star was rising and we along with it. Because I talked proper when I needed to and didnГўВҖВҷt mind wearing a suit, I soon found myself working directly with several favored customers. YouГўВҖВҷd probably even know the names of a couple of them, but you wonГўВҖВҷt hear them from me. When I landed a narc as a customer, we had a nice celebration. Portland cops had worked real hard to clean up, so getting inside news was a real score. He got his shit for free, sometimes wrapped in Benjamins, and we heard rumors nobody else heard.  ThatГўВҖВҷs how, when Lamonte got busted, I had his job. Lamonte was true to his word and didnГўВҖВҷt name names. And Christine didnГўВҖВҷt ask questions about my import/export business and cried when she thought I wasnГўВҖВҷt looking. I was a ГўВҖВңdistrict managerГўВҖВқ and the account managers reported directly to me. I didnГўВҖВҷt change nothin'. What worked for Lamonte worked for me.   Now.  I open the envelope right away this time. A single sheet of paper. A single word. ГўВҖВңAaron.ГўВҖВқ Written on the back of the envelope was ГўВҖВңGisele,ГўВҖВқ in GiseleГўВҖВҷs handwriting.  Of course it wasnГўВҖВҷt Gisele. She died years ago.  IГўВҖВҷm staring at the envelope and I dimly remember that like Angela and myself, she had a different name. How many people knew that? Sure, she had different reasons, but still. She was buried years ago and now I have this envelope in my hand and a sheet of paper which reads ГўВҖВңAaron.ГўВҖВқ   Then.  I met Aaron again shortly before my second bust. I was pulling down a cool seven grand a month. I never skimmed, I didnГўВҖВҷt cut the dope too much and when I fired a dealer for using, I gave them severance pay out of the expense account. Then I told ГўВҖВҳem to get lost.  One day on an exchange, Big Boss told me to carry. We were dealing with some new boys. They were unknown, but they had lots to move. Normally I donГўВҖВҷt hold no truck with guns, but when the big boss trusts you to watch his back and youГўВҖВҷre looking to move up, you do what youГўВҖВҷre told. So I carried. And there, in an old warehouse, was Aaron providing muscle for the new boys.  I slowed down. I guess the big boss sensed something was up and he slowed down, too. Then they slowed down.
