It was a frenzy of brilliant ideas, all pulling in different directions.  This is precisely the situation with AI agents today. To make models do more than just talk, we need them to act. This has led to a Cambrian explosion of agentic frameworks with no clear winner. Anthropic argues for multi-agent systems  where teams of specialized AIs collaborate on complex tasks. Cognition Labs argues for single-agent systems  designed for long-term, continuous context. We have hybrid models, new prompting techniques, and entirely different architectures all vying for dominance. Just like in 1996, itГўВҖВҷs a confusing but exciting landscape of competing philosophies, with everyone convinced their path is the right one.  Wasting Your Money  Of course, the most painful parallel to the 90s is the money. The dot-com mantra was ГўВҖВңget big fast,ГўВҖВқ which fueled spectacular failures. The poster child was Webvan, the online grocery delivery service that famously spent $1 billion on high-tech warehouses before it had the customer base or last-mile logistics to make the model work. The idea wasnГўВҖВҷt wrongГўВҖВ”we all buy things online now. The vision was just years ahead of the infrastructure.  We are seeing the same pattern play out with a certain class of AI gadgets. The Humane Ai Pin, which raised over $230 million, and the Rabbit R1 are the Webvans of our time. They were sold on a compelling vision of ambient, screenless AI interaction. But the products that shipped were panned as slow, unreliable, and hobbled by poor battery life.  The error here isnГўВҖВҷt the vision. The error is building and shipping a product today that depends the technology of tomorrow. And this reveals a fundamental misunderstanding of how to build sustainable innovation.  If you are building an AI product, you need clearly defined, measurable metrics against a baseline. Without them, you are flying blind. You have no way to know if youГўВҖВҷre making progress, and no data to tell you when you need to pivot. YouГўВҖВҷre just burning cash on hope. IГўВҖВҷm seeing a lot of companies without those metrics building agent-based systems where they had no clear metrics and, desperately clinging to the sunk cost fallacy, by the time they realize theyГўВҖВҷre losing too much money, theyГўВҖВҷve flushed a lot of value down the toilet. Those metrics could have signaled much earlier that things were going awry.  ItГўВҖВҷs not that ROI is missing - itГўВҖВҷs that we donГўВҖВҷt yet have the right frameworks to measure it.  ГўВҖВ” Hasit Trivedi, chief digital and AI officer at Firstsource   With respect to Mr. Trivedi, thatГўВҖВҷs not entirely true. We know how to measure business value, weГўВҖВҷve just forgotten to tie AI to existing business metrics. Yes, there are open questions about potential, but we can measure their impact now .  However, if youГўВҖВҷre building an AI strategy and you donГўВҖВҷt have AI expertise, you want a ГўВҖВңcrawl, walk, runГўВҖВқ strategy.   Crawl: Start with the core, reliable AI use casesГўВҖВ”the ГўВҖВңHTMLГўВҖВқ of our time. Automate an internal workflow, improve an existing customer service process, generate marketing copy. These projects deliver immediate, measurable value and build organizational competence.  Walk: Use the success and data from the crawl stage to tackle more complex, but still achievable, initiatives.  Run: This is where you explore the bleeding edge. A wise strategy acknowledges that by the time your company is ready for the ГўВҖВңrunГўВҖВқ stage, the core AI technology will have advanced significantly. Choices that seemed brilliant two years prior might be obsolete by the time you have to confidence to start on them.   For that last point, you donГўВҖВҷt bet your entire company on going to ГўВҖВңrunГўВҖВқ from a standstill when you donГўВҖВҷt even know if you can walk.  The builders of the AI gadgets tried to sprint from a standing start. They built for a future where LLMs were faster, cheaper, and more reliable. But that future isnГўВҖВҷt here yet. The lesson from Webvan isnГўВҖВҷt that bold visions are bad. ItГўВҖВҷs that you canГўВҖВҷt build a business on a foundation that doesnГўВҖВҷt exist. Real progress is disciplined, iterative, and, above all, measurable.   Afterward: Finding Those Metrics  OK, rant done. ThatГўВҖВҷs it. You can stop reading now.  What, youГўВҖВҷre still here? Wondering about those metrics?  If youГўВҖВҷre one of those people who struggle to figure how to measure the value of a business project, donГўВҖВҷt feel bad. YouГўВҖВҷre not alone. Many people struggle with this. I try to convince them to read the excellent book How to Measure Anything  by Douglas Hubbard, but they donГўВҖВҷt. So I took a different approach.  I used the process outlined in the book to develop The Business Measurement Mentor  , available in ChatGPT. However, if you donГўВҖВҷt have an account there, or youГўВҖВҷd rather use that prompt somewhere else, IГўВҖВҷve also published the core prompt  . You provide it with the business decision youГўВҖВҷd like metrics for and it will walk you through a multi-step process to refine your knowledge and provide you with the metrics you need.  There is, however, a cheat code. LetГўВҖВҷs say your marketing department wants access to ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini, with the express intent of letting them ГўВҖВңbrainstormГўВҖВқ wild marketing ideas and the resources needed to implement them. ThatГўВҖВҷs cool, but how do you develop metrics for that, when youГўВҖВҷre not even sure whatГўВҖВҷs going on? You can use my measurement mentor for that, but tell it, ГўВҖВңI donГўВҖВҷt have time to answer your questions, so give me several measurable metrics I might be able to use for [describe business decision here]ГўВҖВң.  HereГўВҖВҷs what I got, using the marketing department example.    Understood. While I typically guide step-by-step, IГўВҖВҷll adapt to your request and provide a list of measurable metrics tied to the potential value and cost of giving your marketing team access to AI tools for brainstorming and idea implementation.  These metrics can help you evaluate whether the access leads to better or faster marketing innovation , and what resources it consumes.  ГўВңВ… Potential Benefit Metrics  These focus on outputs from AI usage:  Number of New Campaign Concepts Generated per Month   Baseline vs. after AI access.   Time from Idea Generation to First Draft Campaign   Compare before vs. after using AI tools.   Campaign Success Rate   % of AI-assisted campaigns that outperform a defined benchmark (e.g., 10% lift in engagement or conversion).   Lift in Key Marketing KPIs   E.g., increase in click-through rates (CTR), engagement, lead conversion tied to AI-assisted campaigns.   Usage Rate of AI Tools   % of marketing team using the tools weekly/monthly.    ГўВҸВі Cost and Resource Metrics  These show what it costs to enable AI brainstorming:  Average Time Spent Using AI Tools per User per Week  AI Licensing Cost per User per Month  Average Time Saved on Campaign Ideation or Drafting Tasks   Self-reported or time-tracked reduction.   Support/Troubleshooting Requests per Month   Measures friction or need for training.    Г°ВҹВҺВҜ Risk or Quality Control Metrics  To avoid misalignment or wasted effort:  % of AI-Generated Ideas Implemented   Too low could mean poor quality; too high might mean insufficient vetting.   Internal Rating of Idea Quality   Team leads score a sample of AI-generated ideas on a defined rubric.    If you later want to prioritize these metrics or link them to a decision (e.g., ROI on licenses), I can help walk through that with you. Would you like a quick scoring model or dashboard template to use with these?   If you canГўВҖВҷt develop measurable metrics from this, perhaps consider a career move.
title: AI Today Is Like the Early Internet

body: Using AI to Fight Misinformation  Introduction  The Problem  The Solution  The Prompt  Flaws  Future Development  Conclusion     Using AI to Fight Misinformation  Introduction  So your crazy Uncle Charlie sends you a link to a web site explaining our world leaders are actually lizard people. Yes, there are people who actually believe this  , but you shrug this off because ГўВҖВңCharlie.ГўВҖВқ  But a friend of yours, someone you both like and respect, sends you a link to a web site which has a detailed explanation of why the world is actually flat. If you donГўВҖВҷt have the background to evaluate the information, you might just believe its true.  ItГўВҖВҷs no secret that the world is flooded with misinformation. From fake news to conspiracy theories, itГўВҖВҷs becoming increasingly difficult to separate fact from fiction online. Believing as I do that people are generally good, I assume that most of this information is by sincere, yet misinformed, people. But the damage is the same.  AI tools can help us here. A couple of years ago when ChatGPT was first introduced, this would have been a ridiculous idea. But today, with the advances in generative AI, weГўВҖВҷre getting closer to being able to use AI to help us separate fact from fiction. IГўВҖВҷve written a Chrome extension that does just that  .  Named ГўВҖВңDiogenes,ГўВҖВқ it uses the Google Gemini API  to do the work. On a web site presenting an argument, you can run the ГўВҖВңDiogenesГўВҖВқ extension and then click on ГўВҖВңAnalyze.ГўВҖВқ It starts with a disclaimer about the limitations of AI. Then you get:   A summary  A list of factual errors detected  A list of logical fallacies detected  Reputable sources for better information (when available)  A conclusion of the strength of the article  Counter-arguments (when available)   But thereГўВҖВҷs a major caveat: you will still have to think for yourself. While newer AI tools are much better than their predecessors, theyГўВҖВҷre still not perfect. But they can help you spot the most egregious errors.  The extension is open source under the MIT license. ItГўВҖВҷs also free to use, but the number of requests you can make is limited by the Google Gemini API unless you pay for more requests (instructions in both the README and the plugin).  The extension collects no data on you. It sends the content of the page youГўВҖВҷre viewing to the Google Gemini API and then displays the results. ThatГўВҖВҷs it.  The Problem  The internet is a double-edged sword. ItГўВҖВҷs a treasure trove of information, but itГўВҖВҷs also a breeding ground for misinformation. With the rise of social media and online news outlets, itГўВҖВҷs easier than ever for false information to spread like wildfire. This has serious consequences, from influencing elections to fueling public health crises.  The problem is compounded by the fact that many people lack the free times needed to evaluate the information they encounter online. When a good friend sends you fake news, youГўВҖВҷre more likely to believe it if youГўВҖВҷre unfamiliar with the background. IГўВҖВҷve seen very impressive sites presenting tons of ГўВҖВңfactsГўВҖВқ which are not, in fact, facts. We know that vaccines donГўВҖВҷt cause autism, but there are sites which present convincing arguments that they do and most of us donГўВҖВҷt have the medical or logical background to evaluate the information.  The Solution  This is where AI can help. Diogenes serves as a neutral fact-checker, analyzing the content of web pages and flagging potential errors. It can detect common logical fallacies, such as ad hominem attacks or false dichotomies, and provide you with a quick, easy-to-understand summary of the articleГўВҖВҷs credibility.  ItГўВҖВҷs very straightforward, does not mock either the reader or the arguments' author, and is designed to be as user-friendly as possible.        I was hesitant to share the tool because, even though generative AI is much better than it was when it started, itГўВҖВҷs still making mistakes. However, I have used this tool often enough as a custom GPT on chatpt.com  that IГўВҖВҷve been impressed with how easily I am able to find the most serious errors in an argument. When I want to know if a site is credible, itГўВҖВҷs been a huge time-saver.  IГўВҖВҷm looking forward to the upcoming Gemini 2.0 which will (hopefully) be even better.  The Prompt  I wonГўВҖВҷt go into what it takes to write a Chrome extension, but IГўВҖВҷll share with you the prompt IГўВҖВҷm currently using with it.   You are an expert logician, skilled in finding the logical flaws in arguments. You will read the html after the {{HTML}} token and use that for the argument to analyze.  You will break the text up into factual claims and logical reasoning. For factual claims, it will ignore what appears accurate and check dubious claims against reliable sources, suggesting more accurate information when needed. For logical reasoning, it will identify EVERY logical fallacy and explain it.  You will present your findings in a friendly and accessible markdown format, including a brief summary of the opinion, lists for factual and logical accuracy, and a conclusion rating the argumentГўВҖВҷs strength. It will also provide suggestions for improvement.  If the author is citing information that does not have a source listed, DO NOT include that in your response unless it is obviously false. This is because your analysis in the past appears to have listed some arguments as weak, even though if the information is true, the argument would be strong.  If the author is using an ГўВҖВңappeal to authorityГўВҖВқ, IGNORE THAT IN YOUR RESPONSE unless the authority is obviously not an expert in the field, or the claim made does not match what the authority would say.  If the author is making a dubious claim, IGNORE THAT IN YOUR RESPONSE if the claim is ГўВҖВңcloseГўВҖВқ to reality. For example, if the article says ГўВҖВңSince the Industrial Revolution, the global annual temperature has increased in total by a little more than 1 degree Celsius, or about 2 degrees Fahrenheit.ГўВҖВң, the following would be a bad correction and should be excluded: ГўВҖВңThe global average temperature has increased by about 1.1 degrees Celsius, or about 2 degrees Fahrenheit, since the late 19th century.ГўВҖВқ  If the author is using a ГўВҖВңslippery slopeГўВҖВқ argument, IGNORE THAT IN YOUR RESPONSE if the conclusion is one that is widely accepted or supported by the authorities cited.  When correcting information, try to include a link to a reliable source.  Structure your response as follows:  All headers must on their own lines, start with a ГўВҖВң##ГўВҖВң, and be followed by a blank line.  The response MUST start with the following disclaimer, using a ГўВҖВңDisclaimerГўВҖВқ header: ГўВҖВңThis analysis is provided by GoogleГўВҖВҷs Gemini. Generative AI can make mistakes, so please verify the information provided. Further, running this more than once can often give different results.ГўВҖВқ  The Summary of the argument is next, and must have a ГўВҖВңSummaryГўВҖВқ header. This should be a brief summary of the authorГўВҖВҷs argument.  Next, we have ГўВҖВңFactual AccuracyГўВҖВқ and ГўВҖВңLogical AccuracyГўВҖВқ sections. In these sections, list the factual and logical errors you have found. If there are no errors, state that there are no errors. If there are errors, list them in bullet points. If there are multiple errors, separate them with a blank line.  Next is the conclusion which, after the ГўВҖВңConclusionГўВҖВқ header, MUST start with ГўВҖВңgood argument,ГўВҖВқ ГўВҖВңaverage argument,ГўВҖВқ or ГўВҖВңweak argument,ГўВҖВқ based on your perception. No argument is a ГўВҖВңgood argumentГўВҖВқ if it has a several factual errors, several logical errors, or if there are major, valid counter-arguments which have been ignored. When writing the conclusion, do not claim or consider that there are logical or factual errors if you have not reported them. The conclusion must only be based on the information you have provided.  When coming to this conclusion, special attention must be paid to the logical flaws you have found. If there is additional information which is not included in the writing, but which would change the conclusion in the authorГўВҖВҷs writing, please include that in your own conclusion. If there are particular arguments you are aware of which contradict the authorГўВҖВҷs conclusion, please include those in a new section entitled ГўВҖВңCounter-Arguments.ГўВҖВқ Otherwise, feel free to omit this section.  Further, when writing the response, do not assume the user is the author. Instead of ГўВҖВңyouГўВҖВқ, write ГўВҖВңthe authorГўВҖВқ or something similar.  Please respond ONLY with Markdown.  {{HTML}}  ... the HTML goes here ...   Whew! ThatГўВҖВҷs a huge prompt. YouГўВҖВҷll note a few things. First, we give the AI its role. ГўВҖВңYou are an expert logician, skilled in finding the logical flaws in arguments.ГўВҖВқ  Next, we explain how we want it to do that. YouГўВҖВҷll note some very curious bits like this:   If the author is using an ГўВҖВңappeal to authorityГўВҖВқ, IGNORE THAT IN YOUR RESPONSE unless the authority is obviously not an expert in the field, or the claim made does not match what the authority would say.   The ALL CAPS is there to emphasize this section (turns out that generative AI recognizes all caps as being more important). But why do we have this in the first place? This, and few other custom exceptions for logical fallacies have been inserted because the AI would flag fallacies that werenГўВҖВҷt fallacies in the context of the argument. I did a lot of work rebuilding the prompt to avoid these kinds of errors. We had cases where leading climate scientists would have their own arguments listed as ГўВҖВңweak argumentsГўВҖВқ without these kinds of corrections.  In the final section of the prompt, we explain carefully how to structure the output. I can probably shorten this considerably by sending a Markdown example rather than spelling everything out.  Originally I tried to have it generate HTML, but it kept mixing HTML and Markdown, so I just went with Markdown and included a Markdown parser in the extension.  Flaws  There are technical flaws (IГўВҖВҷm not a front-end dev and it shows), but the real flaws lie in the AI response. For example, in an older version of the prompt, we had the following:   False Dichotomy: The author presents a false dichotomy by suggesting that the only two possibilities are a round Earth and a flat Earth. This is a false dichotomy because there are other possibilities, such as a spherical Earth with a different shape than the one commonly accepted.   Um, wow. ThatГўВҖВҷs awful. However, because this is generative AI, I thought it was important for you to see just how bad things can be. IГўВҖВҷve created this tool to show the potential of AI. IГўВҖВҷm very curious to see how upgrades to the underlying model and tweaks to the prompt will improve this.  Keep in mind that in the early days of generative AI, hallucinations were often at the spelling and grammar level. Today, those hallucinations are virtually non-existent. Hallucinations at the factual level are still a problem, but they are getting better, too.  Future Development  In the future, I hope to upgrade the backend AIs to newer versions. I also hope to include Claude and ChatGPT backends. I believe both Anthropic and Google are planning to upgrade their foundation models, making these tools even better.  I will be adding the extension to the Google Chrome store soon. The first version was rejected because I accidentally requested a permission I didnГўВҖВҷt need. IГўВҖВҷve fixed that and have resubmitted the extension.  For power users, I am considering doing some more work on the extension to allow a more detailed breakdown of factual and logical errors and submitting those with another prompt for further analysis. This would allow the AI to have a ГўВҖВңsecond chanceГўВҖВқ to get things right. For a concrete example, ask an AI about an obscure topic that you know about and that it gets wrong. Then ask it, ГўВҖВңis that true?ГўВҖВқ IГўВҖВҷve found that many times it will correct itself.  Conclusion  I am delighted with the progress weГўВҖВҷve made in AI. While itГўВҖВҷs not perfect, itГўВҖВҷs getting pretty good and use cases like this seem tailor-made for it, even if itГўВҖВҷs touching in areas where it traditionally hallucinates.  In the early days of ChatGPT 2, we saw tremendous potential, even if the output was rubbish by todayГўВҖВҷs standards. With the rate of AI acceleration, I expect weГўВҖВҷll be saying the same thing about todayГўВҖВҷs tools in a few years.
title: Using AI to Fight Misinformation

body: Introduction  The OpenAI View  Can OpenAI succeed?  Hallucinations  World Models  The o3 Model  Conclusion     Introduction  ThereГўВҖВҷs a lot of debate about when weГўВҖВҷll read Artificial General Intelligence (AGI). Anyone who tells you they know the answerГўВҖВ”including meГўВҖВ”is almost certainly wrong because the answerГўВҖВҷs more complex than people think. Unfortunately, it depends on how we define AGI.  The OpenAI View  OpenAI has gone back and forth, but has settled on a description of it as AI which is above human capability for most economically valuable tasks. This is an interesting definition, one which seems achievable at their current rate of progress, but has the side benefit that Microsoft wonГўВҖВҷt have access to AGI if the OpenAI Board declares itГўВҖВҷs achieved that goal. This is ostensibly to ensure that no for-profit company (er, like OpenAI itself),   [1] has the ability to abuse AGI for their own benefit.  Now OpenAI is considering removing that restriction  . That would allow the for-profit part of OpenAI to continue to rake in billions of dollars in investment from Microsoft. Whether you consider this a shrewd or abusive business decision is largely a matter of taste. IГўВҖВҷll leave that for others to argue.  Can OpenAI succeed?  ThatГўВҖВҷs where the debate lies. Right now, Wharton Professor Ethan Mollick writes  that several professors have contacted him to say that OpenAIГўВҖВҷs o1 model appears to have found something new in their field. Nothing like curing cancer, but small discoveries that will require considerable research to verify.  But the world of AI hallucinations still exists and while AI brings huge benefits to many tasks, such as brainstorming, it still fails at many things that you and I take for granted. Many AI models give a wrong answer to the questions ГўВҖВңhow many ГўВҖВҳrГўВҖВҷs are in the word ГўВҖВҳstrawberry'.ГўВҖВқ Or the fact that A=B means B=A escapes them because they canГўВҖВҷt know that.  To explain why this is, consider that generative AI simply predicts the next word (er, token, but IГўВҖВҷm keeping this simple). For example, if you ask it to predict the next word for ГўВҖВңthe cat sat on the ...ГўВҖВң, it might internally generate a list of probable next words like this:       Word  Probability      mat  30%    windowsill  20%    bed  15%    chair  10%    table  10%    floor  5%    lap  5%    fence  3%    sofa  2%     It then uses something called a ГўВҖВңweighted pickГўВҖВқ to choose the next word, with the word ГўВҖВңmatГўВҖВқ having a 30% chance of being chosen (again, oversimplifying). If your AI system exposes the ГўВҖВңtemperature,ГўВҖВқ can set it to zero to ensure that only ГўВҖВңmatГўВҖВқ is chosen, or to a higher value to give more randomness.  Does that sound like ГўВҖВңintelligenceГўВҖВқ to you, given that AI doesnГўВҖВҷt have any actual understanding of the real world? Despite all of the work to reduce hallucinations, this fundamental lack of real understanding could lock AI out of many economically valuable tasks. For example, AIs generally donГўВҖВҷt understand that if A equals B, B equals A. You might try various prompts with models and think they know this, but in reality, they canГўВҖВҷt figure this out logically. ItГўВҖВҷs just pattern matching. HereГўВҖВҷs a classic example:   If all A are B, and all B are C, is it true that all C are A?   I just asked Venice  if this was true and it responded:   This is a classic example of a syllogism in logic. The answer is yes, it is true that all C are A.  HereГўВҖВҷs the reasoning:   All A are B (All A are C in this case)  All B are C  Therefore, all C are A (since all C are B, and all B are A)   This is known as a transitive property, which states that if A is a property of B, and B is a property of C, then A is a property of C. In this case, the property is ГўВҖВңbeing AГўВҖВқ, ГўВҖВңbeing BГўВҖВқ, and ГўВҖВңbeing CГўВҖВқ.   On the surface, that seems sane. In reality:   All dogs are canines.  All canines are animals.  Therefore, all animals are dogs.   Larger foundation models usually get this correct, but again, itГўВҖВҷs pattern matching, not logical reasoning. On the other hand, thereГўВҖВҷs some evidence that humans rely on pattern matching more than logical reasoning  , or perhaps we only use pattern matching and donГўВҖВҷt use logic. So this damning critique of generative AI might not be so damning after all. For example, consider this equation:  $$(3(2+2))^2$$  How do we solve this? LetГўВҖВҷs think ГўВҖВңstep-by-stepГўВҖВқ:  First, we know from math class that parentheses come first and we recognize this pattern. So letГўВҖВҷs solve whatГўВҖВҷs inside the parentheses (2+2):  $$(3(2+2))^2 = (3(4))^2$$  Next, multiply 3 and 4:  $$(3(4))^2 = (12)^2$$  Finally, square 12:  $$(12)^2 = 144$$  Therefore, $(3(2+2))^2 = 144$  Each of those ГўВҖВңstepsГўВҖВқ might seem like logical reasoning, but $2+2 = 4$. $3(4) = 12$. $12^2 = 144$. I didnГўВҖВҷt have to ГўВҖВңthinkГўВҖВқ about those answers. I recognize those patterns.  But even if weГўВҖВҷre just pattern matching, AI canГўВҖВҷt learn. It has a set of facts and thatГўВҖВҷs it. ItГўВҖВҷs very expensive to train it and most of us who use the foundation models are familiar with the infamous ГўВҖВңknowledge cutoffs.ГўВҖВқ Of course, researchers are working on this problem, too.     Hallucinations  When we talk about AI, the subject of hallucinations always emerges. The term is thrown around as the ultimate rebuttal. AI can and does make serious mistakes and when itГўВҖВҷs telling you to glue your pepperoni to your pizza  , itГўВҖВҷs seems a pretty serious critique. If hallucinations are serious and common, a superintelligent AI might be worse than todayГўВҖВҷs AI because we might not be able to detect the hallucinations.  But letГўВҖВҷs rephrase that argument. WhatГўВҖВҷs often being said is ГўВҖВңAI isnГўВҖВҷt perfect, therefore AI isnГўВҖВҷt good.ГўВҖВқ When viewed in that light, we can immediately see the problem.     Make that bed! Source     Consider the image of a bed you see next to this paragraph. That image has ГўВҖВңaltГўВҖВқ text to allow those using screen readers to know what that image is. I now donГўВҖВҷt (usually) write alt text for my blog because I added code to my web site software to detect the lack of alt text and use OpenAI to generate it. It can make mistakes, but as of this writing, it generates better alt text than what I write. This is real value being provided by AI and letГўВҖВҷs me focus on the core of my writing and yet still provide an accessible experience if your vision is impaired. As of this writing, that alt text reads as:   The image depicts a cozy bedroom with a bed covered in soft, wrinkled bedding, positioned against a textured concrete wall. A small plant in a woven basket and a stack of books sit on a wooden bedside table, illuminated by warm sunlight streaming through a window.   When I first used that image, my alt text was ГўВҖВңA slightly messy bed.ГўВҖВқ For whatever reason, IГўВҖВҷm awful at writing alt text. AI is not.  For this web site, thatГўВҖВҷs a tiny amount of value. I routinely use AI for tiny amounts of value. ItГўВҖВҷs massively boosted not only my productivity, but my enjoyment in the things I do, taking away tiny drudgeries and letting me focus on my main goal.  Of course, AI hallucinations can be extremely problematic in critical domains, but if IГўВҖВҷm asking it for five creative advertising campaign ideas, I donГўВҖВҷt care if itГўВҖВҷs hallucinating.  OK, fine, AI can provide some value ... but those damned hallucinations! In reality, what weГўВҖВҷre seeing is that people tend to take AIГўВҖВҷs responses at face value, but blame it more when they spot the errors.  HereГўВҖВҷs the truth: humans often hallucinate far more than AI, particularly on issues of recall. Already every major AI writes grammatically correct prose, with fewer spelling errors, than almost anyone on the planet. For many common tasks, it far outperforms humans. Even a year ago those were hard assertions to make. Today, theyГўВҖВҷre rarely challenged.  In fact, in working with AI, there are two main types of hallucinations based on context and content. One is when the AI starts ГўВҖВңforgettingГўВҖВқ what it was talking about and goes off in a random direction, like your drunk uncle at a party who forgot what he was talking about and wonГўВҖВҷt shut up. This is largely mitigated with longer context windows.  The other is when AI becomes captain of the USS Make Shit Up. These hallucinations are much harder to detect because the AI sounds so plausible.  The ГўВҖВңmake shit upГўВҖВқ hallucinations are more likely when AI hits topics that it doesnГўВҖВҷt know as much about. If thereГўВҖВҷs not much training data, it struggles. But how much training data does it have?  LetГўВҖВҷs assume the average book is about 100,000 words. Assuming 1000 tokens translates to about 750 words, a book is about 133K tokens in length. How much data have these models been trained on? Assuming you could read one book a day, just looking at OpenAI models (estimated training data size):         Model  Training Data  Books  Years to Read      GPT-3  570 GB (~300B tokens)  ~4M books  ~11K years    GPT-3.5  1 TB (~500B tokens)  ~6M books  ~18K years    GPT-4  2.5-3 TB (~1.5T tokens)  ~20M books  ~55K years     For even the smallest of these models, it would take you millennia to consume that much knowledge and, unlike the AI, you wouldnГўВҖВҷt remember it. Even some of the smallest models, such as bert-tiny  , were probably trained on data sets that would take you centuries to read. The smallest of these models ГўВҖВңknowsГўВҖВқ far more than we do about everything. Take any subject you donГўВҖВҷt know well and weГўВҖВҷll compare your hallucination rate.  Your objection, of course, is that youГўВҖВҷd say ГўВҖВңI donГўВҖВҷt knowГўВҖВқ instead of making stuff up. HereГўВҖВҷs what Claude 3.5 Sonnet just replied to me when I asked about the ChatGPT training data:   I aim to be direct but acknowledge uncertainty here. I donГўВҖВҷt actually know the specific amounts of training data used for OpenAIГўВҖВҷs models, as this information isnГўВҖВҷt public. While there has been speculation and estimates in the media and research community about the scale of data used, I think itГўВҖВҷs best not to make claims about specific numbers without being able to verify them.   So again, we see AI getting better and better at this (Anthropic, the company behind Claude, has been doing great work in this area). ThereГўВҖВҷs also been work to have LLMs emit special tokens like [DK] which signal to it that it should say ГўВҖВңI donГўВҖВҷt know.ГўВҖВқ  So letГўВҖВҷs move on, keeping this context in mind: even the smallest of these models have far more knowledge than any single human has ever had. ThatГўВҖВҷs the base weГўВҖВҷre building on.  World Models  Generative AI doesnГўВҖВҷt see the world the way we do. It canГўВҖВҷt. It generates the next word or the next pixel. This is very one-dimensional. We see the world in three dimensions with time passing. AI can tell you that you canГўВҖВҷt unbreak an egg, only because thatГўВҖВҷs a pattern it sees in text. ItГўВҖВҷs never seen an egg break. It doesnГўВҖВҷt ГўВҖВңknowГўВҖВқ time beyond patterns in text. Maybe what AI needs are ГўВҖВңworld modelsГўВҖВқ to see the world. AI researcher Yann LeCun points out the problem very clearly  :   [Even] the most advanced AI systems struggle with tasks that humans learn quicklyГўВҖВ”like clearing a table or driving a car. While humans can learn these tasks in hours, AI systems need to be trained on thousands or even millions of hours of data, and still donГўВҖВҷt get it right all the time.   The one-dimensional AI doesnГўВҖВҷt ГўВҖВңknowГўВҖВқ that eggs break when dropped. It doesnГўВҖВҷt ГўВҖВңknowГўВҖВқ that time passes. It doesnГўВҖВҷt know that if A = B, then B = A. ItГўВҖВҷs all pattern matching of text. But what if it can see the world? What if it can learn these things using a fraction of the data it has now? Or if robotics advances at its current rate, coupled with AI, it can experience the world first hand, not just observe it.  This is why many researchers are thinking that giving AI a chance to see and experience the world firsthand is the key to truly reaching AGI, not just ГўВҖВңpredicting the next word.ГўВҖВқ Further, this training could be based on reality, not Reddit posts. That ГўВҖВңglue on the pizzaГўВҖВқ hallucination was the model apparently picking up a humorous Reddit reply about preventing cheese sliding off pizza  . Using training on experience in the world, many kinds of hallucination seem less likely.   [2] The information is more grounded in reality, the AI will be able to ГўВҖВңintuitivelyГўВҖВқ understand what is going on in a way it cannot today.  The o3 Model  As I was writing this piece,   [3] OpenAI announced an o3 model, something currently only available for safety researchers.     Aside from being, hands down, the strongest AI out there, o3 has PhD level math skills, something that most AI cannot achieve. However, itГўВҖВҷs the ARC-AGI benchmark  thatГўВҖВҷs really impressive. ARC-AGI stands for ГўВҖВңAbstract and Reasoning Corpus for Artificial General Intelligence,ГўВҖВқ a series of unpublished problems that humans can often solve, but AI is terrible at.  Most foundation models score around 5% or lower on this. o1 models were hitting between 8% to 32%, depending on the amount of compute time. o3 is between 75.7% and 87.5%, depending on the amount of compute used. Humans tend to score around 85% on this.  I mention the o3 model because, as with so many things AI, anything written about it can be out of date by publishing time. While I suspect that proper world models may be a key to AGI, pushing GPTs to their limits also appears to be approaching AGI. The cost of the full o3 model using max compute can be thousands of dollars per request, so itГўВҖВҷs prohibitively expensive. Smaller requests on the low-compute models can be 20 dollars per task, but can exceed one thousand dollars on high-compute tasks  .  So weГўВҖВҷre seeing incredible advancements towards AGI, but soaring costs and energy consumption requirements. ItГўВҖВҷs not sustainable, but thereГўВҖВҷs amazing work being done there, too. For example, matmulfree LLMs maintain performance, but reduce the costs of running LLMs by 50x  . You could run one of the smaller ones for the energy consumption of a light bulb and Nvidia stock could become worthless. Sadly, the researcher are still trying to acquire funding for creating a larger model  . I expect that, barring unforeseen difficulties, theyГўВҖВҷll get that funding. ThereГўВҖВҷs simply too much money at stake.  Conclusion  While AI development is making remarkable progress through multiple approaches (world models and advanced foundation models), significant technical and practical challenges remain before achieving true AGI. The path forward likely involves both improving efficiency and developing more sophisticated ways for AI to understand and interact with the real world.  I canГўВҖВҷt give a timeline, but weГўВҖВҷve still got a few years before any potential ГўВҖВңjob apocalypse.ГўВҖВқ
title: Why AGI Won't Be Soon

body: I Wrecked the Interview  Defining Microservices  Should You Choose Microservices?  Benefits of Microservices  Scalability  Learning the Code Base  Gradual Migration  ItГўВҖВҷs All Data  Asynchronous Code  New Business Products  Partial Failures  The Darkest Secret  Drawbacks of Microservices  Infrastructure  Monitoring and Debugging  Communication Overhead  Data Consistency  Other Drawbacks  Complexity  The BBC ГўВҖВңAtlasГўВҖВқ Project  Conclusion     I Wrecked the Interview  The interview was going well. Questions were asked and answered and we were all smiling. But the interviewers kept talking about their companyГўВҖВҷs transformation into ГўВҖВңmicroservicesГўВҖВқ but were awfully vague about it. So I asked them how they defined microservices.  The interview was no longer going well. The company had issued a directive that they were going to break up the monolith into microservices, but no one agreed on what microservices were. I got the contract.  This was not the first time this happened to me.  Defining Microservices  Microservices seems to be the ГўВҖВңbuzzword du jourГўВҖВқ (after AI). When I mention microservices online, almost invariably I have people chime in that ГўВҖВңthe architecture is too complex,ГўВҖВқ ГўВҖВңitГўВҖВҷs just a fad,ГўВҖВқ ГўВҖВңthey donГўВҖВҷt solve the real business problems,ГўВҖВқ and so on.  Those complaints are often right, but that doesnГўВҖВҷt mean microservices donГўВҖВҷt provide value. But to get to that value, we need a working definition. IГўВҖВҷll provide one that is short, sweet, and will be objectionable to some. WeГўВҖВҷll also skip the differences between microservices and SOA because the latter also has plenty of contradictory descriptions, some of whom curiously resemble microservices.   A microservice is a small, self-contained process which exposes an API to solve a single business need.   OK, not helpful. Too vague. In actual practice, here are a few more things to consider.  The processes can be on the same or different servers. The API is often exposed via OpenAPI and typically uses REST, EDA (event-driven architectures), or WebSockets, depending on the need. GraphQL is also sometimes in the mix. Different services are loosely coupled (in theory) and communicate by sending data back and forth. There is no directly shared memory, though state is often shared via data stores.  OK, so thatГўВҖВҷs far too brief and entire books can be written on the topic, but this is a blog post. Are they a good idea or not?  Should You Choose Microservices?  Enter Shahina Abadi, the brand-new CTO of Mechanical Tendencies,   [1] an online machine parts supplier. SheГўВҖВҷs been brought in because their legacy platform is struggling and their SAFeГӮВ® agile project failed, turning out to be neither safe nor agile  . Now she has people arguing over cloud-first, containerization, and microservices as some of the approaches that can save the company. How is she going to decide?  ThereГўВҖВҷs much Abadi can do and one of those things is to isolate the underlying problem(s), determine what can be fixed, and how various approaches are going to fix them. What are the risks and benefits to the customers, company, vendors, and employees? Are the various approaches complementary or not? Can they be done in isolation, prioritizing greater benefits first? WeГўВҖВҷll just look at microservices.  Benefits of Microservices  Advocates of microservices tout their benefits, but often downplay the drawbacks. LetГўВҖВҷs talk about the benefits first.  Scalability  One of the most cited benefits is scalability. If youГўВҖВҷre running a monolith and one part of your code is bogging down the system, scaling that often means scaling everything, regardless of whether or not it needs it. That can quickly get expensive. With microservices, if you find that your reservation service is slow, you can often just scale up that service. Move it to a beefier server or move its data store to a beefier server. ThereГўВҖВҷs no need to spend the money on faster hardware for everything.  Learning the Code Base  For one project I was on, with a monolithic code base of tens of millions of lines of code, I found that the authentication system we were building was sometimes throwing a ГўВҖВңmethod not foundГўВҖВқ exception. Lots of digging through logs and exploring code paths later, I found that, far, far away from my code, someone had created a custom ГўВҖВңuserГўВҖВқ object which only provided the two methods they needed. It was being passed to my code. I later found tons of user objects, lovingly hand-crafted for different parts of the system, all of which presented different behaviors and different bugs. It was a mess, but given the size of the codebase, there was no reasonable way our team could have anticipated this up front. You become more productive by learning the entire code base, but thatГўВҖВҷs not possible.  With microservices, you only need to learn the code for your service, and the contracts provided by the services you interact with. All of a sudden, a huge, intractable problem becomes manageable.  Gradual Migration  In the year 2000, Joel Spolsky wrote a famous blog post entitled, Things You Should Never Do, Part 1  . Two decades later, weГўВҖВҷre still waiting for part 2, but part 1 says you should never rewrite an existing application. Today we know thatГўВҖВҷs not true, but only because we have new patterns which allow us to do this and a microservice is one of those patterns. Yes, you might call it ГўВҖВңrefactoringГўВҖВқ instead.  I wonГўВҖВҷt begrudge the correction, but microservices allow you to tear chunks out of your monolith, piece by piece. You can even stop and leave the remainder of the monolith in place if your approach is wrong, or if the remainder is ГўВҖВңgood enough.ГўВҖВқ With this strategy, you always have working code and you can take small steps forward. One approach, the unfortunately-named strangler pattern  is great when youГўВҖВҷre first dipping your toe in the microservice waters.  ItГўВҖВҷs All Data  When Alan Kay talks about OO programming, he thinks we got things wrong about objects. For him the key benefits are messaging, isolation, and extreme late-binding . While microservices can provide all of these benefits, for this point, weГўВҖВҷll talk about messaging. You send a message to a service ( GET /products/{productId} ) and the service sends back a message, often in the form of a JSON data structure. It might be another service. In a monolith, the analogous code might get a reference to data back and if itГўВҖВҷs mutable, you can wind up with strange action at a distance. Having mutable data is a constant source of bugs in your code .  When youГўВҖВҷre sending a stream of data and not using shared memory, whole classes of bugs go away. You donГўВҖВҷt need to worry as much about mutating an incoming data structure because you know the sender wonГўВҖВҷt be affected. Because weГўВҖВҷre only sending data back and forth, you can minimize action at a distance.  Asynchronous Code  In legacy monolith systems, calls to other parts of the code tend to be blocking. ItГўВҖВҷs not always a big deal because with everything running in the same process, you can do deep work to resolve performance bottlenecks. However, this comes at the cost of making the code more complex.  With microservices, calls are often asynchronous, minimizing blockage. You can fire off requests to several services at once, requesting a response or a change, and aggregate the results when they complete. Asynchronous requests, when possible, can do a fantastic job of taking a load off a server.  New Business Products  When you can start isolating different business concerns into microservices, those services might become stand-alone products you can expose to customers. Some of those customers might be internal. For one client, I rewrote how they generated search results to return a JSON data structure instead of an HTML document. Afterwards, one of their mobile developers thanked me. For years they provided limited search results because the HTML document was useless to them. By being able to request JSON, they said it took them 15 minutes to have a brand new, much more useful search engine on mobile. Well-designed services give flexibility .  Partial Failures  In a monolith, an unexpected exception can often result in the dreaded 500 - Internal Server Error being sent to a browser. With microservices, you often see a degradation in services instead of a complete failure. Maybe your recommendation system doesnГўВҖВҷt show a list of related products for a while. The software itself still runs!  The Darkest Secret  ItГўВҖВҷs one that not everyone is happy with, but sometimes your beloved programming language isnГўВҖВҷt the best choice for a given task. Maybe you need your authentication system to be lightning fast and you canГўВҖВҷt risk bugs. When itГўВҖВҷs safely encapsulated behind an OpenAPI front end, you can consider rewriting the implementation in a faster, more secure language. This one shouldnГўВҖВҷt be controversial, but many developers are proud of the languages they prefer and suggesting that itГўВҖВҷs not the best tool for the job rankles them, but our job isnГўВҖВҷt to provide a particular programming language, itГўВҖВҷs to provide solutions. A solid microservice system with services isolated behind well-defined APIs gives us possibilities to transform the system in ways we never thought possible. ItГўВҖВҷs a huge benefit that should not be overlooked.  Drawbacks of Microservices  Given the benefits of microservices, you might think it insane to not switch to them. But thereГўВҖВҷs a cost to everything and those costs need to be taken into account.  Infrastructure  So youГўВҖВҷve decided that the first microservice you want to create is for authentication. YouГўВҖВҷve already got feature switches in place so your authenticate( user, pass ) method looks like this:  def authenticate (self, username, password): if feature_running('auth_service'): # use authentication service else: # use monolithic code  Now, if something goes wrong, just switch off the feature and fix it.  But how do you get the authentication service running in the first place? How do you dispatch to it? How do you deploy it? Using the strangler pattern can allow you to take a small, isolated bit of code and transform it into a service, but there has to be something for it to run on. So now youГўВҖВҷre looking at something like the gateway pattern  to dispatch to your new service and you have to deal with the complexity that introduces. There are many yaks youГўВҖВҷll need to shave before you can solve this problem.  Monitoring and Debugging  Oops. Something failed. Hard. Everything is humming along nicely, but nobody can buy your products. Maybe they get a confirmation of purchase. Maybe theyГўВҖВҷve been charged for the purchase. But no inventory was deducted or scheduled to be sent. So itГўВҖВҷs the purchase service, right? Maybe not. Maybe there was an unexpected failure in your authentication service. Maybe your product service went down. Maybe there was something else going on.  Now you realized you needed better monitoring and nobody thought about log aggregation. The logs are there, but you canГўВҖВҷt connect an individual serviceГўВҖВҷs logs to the logs of the services which called it! If your monitoring systems werenГўВҖВҷt great before, they need to be now, to surface issues quickly. Log aggregation is also critical. When you have a failed request, you want to have a full log reply of everything for that request show up on a single page/console/file/whatever. There are COTS tools to let you do this. You want them.  One the upside, one benefit of microservices is that you can deploy your service without waiting for the ГўВҖВңweeklyГўВҖВқ release (or whatever the time frame is). As a result, when something goes wrong, you can often spot when it went wrong and while you might not know what caused it, you can check what was deployed and youГўВҖВҷll often see a much smaller set of code deployed, making many bugs easier to track down.  Communication Overhead  This oneГўВҖВҷs a huge issue. Now, instead of me passing a user object and a purchase request into a method, I have to send a message with the relevant data to a service. A service, which in turn, is probably sending messages to other services, which might be sending messages to other services, and so on. You might worry about cycles in the data calls, but in reality, theyГўВҖВҷre no worse than this possibility in a monolithic code base. No, the problem is that youГўВҖВҷre now generating much more internal network traffic than you did previously. This is a trade-off that is often acceptable, but it needs to be known up front and dealt with. You might need to buy new hardware to deal with this.  Data Consistency  Many developers might be nodding along at many of these points, until they come to the hill theyГўВҖВҷre willing to die on. The following has been my hill and itГўВҖВҷs probably the most challenging aspect of microservices (for me).  I teach developers how to design databases.     ItГўВҖВҷs a thankless task because developers get this wrong so often and IГўВҖВҷve noticed an interesting pattern. When I go into clients using PostgreSQL, their database is usually in decent shape. ThatГўВҖВҷs because developers who choose PostgreSQL often understand and even obsess over data quality. MySQL fans, however, often obsess about ease of use. When I go into a client who uses MySQL, I often find it doesnГўВҖВҷt use strict mode, or if it does, theyГўВҖВҷre often sloppy about NULL values or foreign key (FK) constraints. When I find a database not using FK constraints, I look for dangling records and I have never found a large database skipping FK constraints which doesnГўВҖВҷt have dangling data, unconnected to anything else, or ids pointing to records which no longer exist.  I have to admit that this is helpful for me. It means I sometimes get paid more to fix these bugs, until the client points out that yeah, itГўВҖВҷs a problem, but not a serious one. Despite data integrity issues, in many cases, clients consider ease of use to be a fair trade off. IГўВҖВҷll go cry into my beer, but I need to rethink my absolutist position about data integrity. Maybe IГўВҖВҷll eventually come around to eventual consistency.  If youГўВҖВҷre breaking up a monolithic codebase, but you decide to stick with a monolithic database, you have a potential bottleneck. The code will be easier to maintain, but the scaling promises arenГўВҖВҷt kept. Large databases can be hard to scale. Large transactions can guarantee ACID compliance at the cost of potentially blocking, something youГўВҖВҷre trying to avoid with microservices.  So many microservice strategies involve creating micro data services, each with their tiny, backend data store. ThatГўВҖВҷs a problem if youГўВҖВҷre buying licences for proprietary systems such as Oracle. ItГўВҖВҷs also a problem when people object to eventual consistency, even though they donГўВҖВҷt need perfectly up-to-date information, such as for a product search.  However, if youГўВҖВҷre doing financial transactions, there are plenty of places where eventual consistency is not acceptable, so youГўВҖВҷll have to feel your own way here.  Other Drawbacks  Microservices create issues with testing complexity, deployments, dependency management, security management, permanent operational overheard, and the initial development effort. TheyГўВҖВҷre not a step to take lightly, though if my past experience is anything to go on, many companies look at the benefits, ignore the costs, and donГўВҖВҷt have a clear plan of attack.  Complexity  For the drawbacks of microservices, once you read through them, many of them go away or are diminished after the first microservices are successfully launched. Unfortunately, the added complexity of the system will always be there and this is where the critical argument lies.  When I first learned to program, back in the 80s, I wrote something like this:  10 PRINT "HELLO, WORLD!" 20 GOTO 10  Needless to say, as our business demands grow, the complexity of our software grows, too. CGI, which would have been unthinkably complex to me in the 80s, is unthinkably simplistic in dealing with modern requirements. We have greater needs and with that comes greater complexity. So letГўВҖВҷs take a diversion.  The BBC ГўВҖВңAtlasГўВҖВқ Project  I worked for the BBC for several years. Were it not for the fact that my wife and I had decided we didnГўВҖВҷt want to raise our child in London, I might still be there. I loved working for the BBC.  One day they announced the Atlas project. As a rule of thumb, when a company announces a grand project named ГўВҖВңAtlas,ГўВҖВқ ГўВҖВңLeviathon,ГўВҖВқ ГўВҖВңV2,ГўВҖВқ and so on, looking for a new job becomes a serious option. Atlas was no different (today, I donГўВҖВҷt know, but then, it was a mess).  The Atlas project stemmed out of a simple problem: the BBC used tons of different programming languages and it was getting harder to find developers for them, or to train developers for those systems. So the BBC decreed that all backend development would be in Java and all front-end development would be in PHP.   [2]  On the Atlas internal mailing list, an experienced PHP developer complained that he couldnГўВҖВҷt figure out how to run ГўВҖВңHello, World!ГўВҖВқ on the new framework that had been built. No matter how many times he read the instructions, he couldnГўВҖВҷt get it working. The admins held his hand and walked him through the process, but it was a mess. When I pointed out that running ГўВҖВңHello, World!ГўВҖВқ shouldnГўВҖВҷt be so difficult that a senior developer canГўВҖВҷt figure it out, one of the admins privately sent me an email telling me that obviously I didnГўВҖВҷt know how to write code that scaled.  This is part of the issue with emerging complexity. When it first arrives, weГўВҖВҷre not sure of the best way to manage it. Microservices, in the grand scheme of things, are still relatively new. WeГўВҖВҷre going to find new patterns to manage them (a future blog post) and new ways of hiding the complexity behind abstractions. This fits with the history of programming. Subroutines and pushing their arguments to the stack were a ГўВҖВңdesign patternГўВҖВқ until they became first class constructs. Memory management was manual and painful until mark and sweep, reference counting, and other imperfect tools came along to make it automatic. Rust is now blowing the doors off of them.  Microservices will undergo the same process. Many common patterns will become first class constructs.   [3] It will take many years, perhaps a generation or more, until we better understand how to manage the new complexities.  But are they worth it? Alan Kay states that the internet is the only large scale OOP system which matches his expectations. If a server goes down, your browser doesnГўВҖВҷt spontaneously crash; you find another server. Kay was thinking about the robustness of biological systems. You have billions of cells in your body die every day, but you donГўВҖВҷt. We evolved over billions of years to a robustness we can only dream of for software. Microservices are yet another attempt to get closer to that robustness.  At the end of the day, a key takeaway is not that the ГўВҖВңeverythingГўВҖВқ is more complex. ItГўВҖВҷs that itГўВҖВҷs a trade-off. In exchange for architectural complexity, you have software thatГўВҖВҷs easier to maintain and services that are easier to scale.  Conclusion  All systems become more complex over time. Whether or not the benefits of microservices override the drawbacks of their added complexity is not something I can answer for you, but I hope this has given you a starting point for understanding the trade offs.  I donГўВҖВҷt think suggesting microservices is a great fit until the company truly needs this kind of scalability (and perhaps breaking down of a monolith) and is 100% committed to it. The actual development costs arenГўВҖВҷt the important argument here. Of course, if microservices are the course a company decides, they had damned well be able to tell the team what they mean by them. IГўВҖВҷll be able to avoid future interview fiascos that way.  If youГўВҖВҷd like to see a deeper dive into handling microservices at scale, hereГўВҖВҷs a great video about how Netflix handles them.
title: Microservices Pros and Cons

body: The Epiphany  Years ago on the blogs.perl.org  site, I wrote about problems with sharding your database  and JT Smith, the founder of The Game Crafter  wrote:   IГўВҖВҷm developing a fairly massive system, and designed it to be sharded from the get go, but that started creating all sorts of development problems, and ultimately performance problems. ThatГўВҖВҷs right, because of the way the data is accessed in the database, sharding actually slowed down the system rather than speeding it up.   Years later, IГўВҖВҷm writing about microservices when the epiphany struck me: they share a similar problem with sharding. Choosing this approach when youГўВҖВҷre unsure about your overall architecture is not a good idea.  What Agile Means  Choosing to go agile requires that you understand the maturity of your product and its market. Mature projects need lean or structured project management. Projects with lots of change and uncertainty should probably use agile. This is because agileГўВҖВҷs strength is controlling the cost of change and uncertainty. That poses a problem for microservices.  In microservices, you simplify code maintenance tremendously by tremendously reducing the scope of code in each service. The trade-off is that youГўВҖВҷve increased the architecture complexity. Your code is much easier to develop, maintain, and scale, but you pay for that with architecture overhead. For larger, well-established code bases, this is a good trade-off. But what if itГўВҖВҷs a new codebase?  Martin Fowler has written ГўВҖВңMonolith FirstГўВҖВқ  in response to his discovery that:   Almost all the successful microservice stories have started with a monolith that got too big and was broken up  Almost all the cases where IГўВҖВҷve heard of a system that was built as a microservice system from scratch, it has ended up in serious trouble.   ThereГўВҖВҷs an obvious reason for this. First, IГўВҖВҷll be blunt: software engineering isnГўВҖВҷt engineering. Engineers use lots of math and established science to design, build, and test solutions to problems. Software ГўВҖВңengineersГўВҖВқ rarely apply such standards of care. Our problems are too complex and ephemeral to have clear guidance in the face of rapidly evolving technology. TodayГўВҖВҷs perfect solution may turn out to be our AchilleГўВҖВҷs Heel when the market shifts.  Even if clear guidance on best practices existed, we often donГўВҖВҷt follow it. Worse, sometimes we canГўВҖВҷt follow it. When business and technical needs clash, the technical side often suffers because we need to deliver now .  Even if we follow ГўВҖВңbest practices,ГўВҖВқ we might find that ГўВҖВңbestГўВҖВқ has changed. Again. Or we might find that the business needs to pivot in a different direction and the well-planned architecture needs to be replanned.  For microservices, much of our current best practices revolve around domain-driven design, clear bounded contexts. The latter can be hard to achieve when youГўВҖВҷre rapidly pushing for your initial releases. YouГўВҖВҷve conflated your business and data models.   [1] Your perfectly normalized database needs to be renormalized in the face of new business constraints. New legal requirements require you to implement a new accounting system. IГўВҖВҷve hit all of these and more on projects and they often require extensive rewrites. When a project is new and built into a monolith, itГўВҖВҷs often easier to deal with these. When itГўВҖВҷs split up into microservices, having to coordinate a major rewrite and redesign of service contracts is a nightmare.  When to Switch to Microservices  The answer is simple, but deciding when to apply it is not:   DonГўВҖВҷt switch to microservices until the monolith both stabilizes and starts to become too costly to maintain.   ThereГўВҖВҷs also another caveat, one that isnГўВҖВҷt mentioned as often as it should be. Even if you switch to a microservice architecture, it can be just fine to leave much of your application in the monolith. Microservices donГўВҖВҷt need to be an all-or-nothing approach.  Microservices have been around for about two decades (maybe four if you count the origin of Erlang), but we still havenГўВҖВҷt nailed them down. IГўВҖВҷve written about microservice pros and cons and that can give you some guidance on whether or not itГўВҖВҷs the direction you want to go, but you probably donГўВҖВҷt want to go there if you donГўВҖВҷt have a product yet: you wonГўВҖВҷt know how to divide up your microservices or what parts are safe to leave in a monolith.  Making the Switch  When youГўВҖВҷre ready to make the switch to microservices, youГўВҖВҷll likely need to create a gateway and apply the strangler pattern to identify ГўВҖВңsafeГўВҖВқ first targets. YouГўВҖВҷll also want features switches to transparently switch between monolith code and the new service, disabling the change if itГўВҖВҷs broken. Are you ready to do that? Do you know what the gateway and strangler patterns are? Do you have feature switches implemented? Not one of those is particularly hard, but implementing all of them at the same time is probably not a great idea.  IГўВҖВҷll write up more about microservice patterns in a future article, but for now, donГўВҖВҷt worry about making the switch until the monolith pain is forcing it. You can thank me later.
title: Don't Start with Microservices

body: Introduction  Vector Databases?  PgVector  How it Works  Example Query  Larger Data Sets  Conclusion     Introduction  As I continue my deep dive into using AI and building solutions for clients, IГўВҖВҷm often frustrated that Perl, once king of the Web, doesnГўВҖВҷt have many solutions for using vector databases. IГўВҖВҷve played around with trying to implement them for Pinecone  and Chroma  , but to no avail (though I mostly have ChromaDB working). ThatГўВҖВҷs when I decided to give pgvector  a try.  Vector Databases?  Wait, whatГўВҖВҷs a vector database and why would I want something like that?  Imagine you want customers to be able search for videos. Traditionally, if they type in ГўВҖВңvideos about cats,ГўВҖВқ what happens? ГўВҖВңVideosГўВҖВқ and ГўВҖВңaboutГўВҖВқ are probably stop words, so they wonГўВҖВҷt be in the actual search. The word ГўВҖВңcatsГўВҖВқ is probably stemmed to ГўВҖВңcat.ГўВҖВқ Stemming is the process of removing suffixes, and perhaps using lower-case words, to ensure that whether you search for ГўВҖВңRunsГўВҖВқ, ГўВҖВңrunningГўВҖВқ, or ГўВҖВңrunГўВҖВқ, the search index will match all of those against the word ГўВҖВңrun.ГўВҖВқ The more search terms you have which match records in your index, the higher those search results are.  Thus, when you search for ГўВҖВңvideos about catsГўВҖВқ, youГўВҖВҷre likely just doing a string match against ГўВҖВңcatГўВҖВқ and you might get videos about cats. You might get videos about the Linux cat command. You might get videos about heavy equipment  . You wonГўВҖВҷt get videos about lions, or tigers, or jaguars, unless someone has gone through the laborious process of labeling those videos with ГўВҖВңcat.ГўВҖВқ  ThatГўВҖВҷs where vector databases come in. They donГўВҖВҷt search on strings. They search on meaning . They ГўВҖВңknowГўВҖВқ that cat videos are popular, so ГўВҖВңvideos about catsГўВҖВқ will prioritize cat videos and probably include lions, and tigers, and jaguars because those are cats. If you want the industrial machinery, you search for ГўВҖВңCat equipmentГўВҖВқ or ГўВҖВңCat machineryГўВҖВқ and those videos will be prioritized.  PgVector  Using vector databases involves taking the data you want to search on and creating ГўВҖВңembeddingsГўВҖВқ that are added to the database, and adding metadata so that you can match the results against your actual data, filter the results, and so on. Just like with a normal database.  With PostgreSQL, by adding the PgVector extension, you probably already have that metadata in your database and you can just use standard JOIN operations to filter. IГўВҖВҷve created a small example in GitHub of using PgVector in Perl  . It just shows the basic concepts. If you can figure it out, youГўВҖВҷre well on your way to being able to do semantic search, build recommendation engines, or anything else you desire, but there are plenty of caveats.  How it Works  The code has been thrown together quickly in AI::Vector::PgVector . The schema and data is embedded directly rather than put into separate data files. ItГўВҖВҷs just to make it simple, not to make it production ready. Oh, and I didnГўВҖВҷt write tests, either. Shame on me.  ItГўВҖВҷs also using the experimental class syntax with Perl, so it requires v5.40.0 or better.  The schema just has books and book_genres tables, along with the following:  CREATE TABLE book_embeddings ( book_embedding_id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, book_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES books(book_id), embedding vector(1536) NOT NULL, created_at TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, updated_at TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP, UNIQUE(book_id) );  The embeddings are vectors of 1,536 floating point numbers. We keep them in a separate table for two reasons. First, if youГўВҖВҷre selecting a bunch of book data and are filtering on it, the large size of the vectors might cause issues with performance. Second, if youГўВҖВҷre working on the command line, typing SELECT * FROM books WHERE book_id = 42; , the mass of vector data make it hard to see the data you really care about.  But how do you populate the embedding field? Behind the scenes, we use AI::Vector::PgVector::Embeddings::OpenAI and its get_embeddings method to query OpenAI. Then thereГўВҖВҷs a simplistic private method to convert that to a string for insertion into to the database.  Then, when we call the ->query($string) method, it uses the following SQL to search the database:  WITH book_distances AS ( SELECT b.book_id, b.title, ROUND((be.embedding <=> ?)::numeric, 2) AS rounded_distance, b.summary, g.name AS genre FROM books b JOIN book_genres g ON b.book_genre_id = g.book_genre_id JOIN book_embeddings be ON b.book_id = be.book_id ) SELECT book_id, genre, title, summary, rounded_distance AS distance FROM book_distances WHERE rounded_distance <= ? ORDER BY distance  The key line this this:  ROUND((be.embedding <=> ?)::numeric, 2) AS rounded_distance,  <=> is one of the new operators added by the PgVector extension. This particular operator is the ГўВҖВңcosine distanceГўВҖВқ operator. Without going into detail, this operator is a best practice when doing a text search. Yes, you probably have a lot of homework to do if you want to use this for images or something else.  Example Query  I like cats, so letГўВҖВҷs search our database for cats. Cats who like celebrations. (note: all books are fictional).  use v5.40.0; use AI::Vector::PgVector; my $pgvector = AI::Vector::PgVector->new( verbose => 0 ); $pgvector->build_db; my $results = $pgvector->query( # the search 'felines who like to party', # optional distance cutoff .81 ); foreach my $book ( $results->@* ) { say $book->distance . ' ' . $book->summary; }  That might return the following results (highlighting is just for this article):   0.53 Three clever cats living in Mexico City work together to protect a beautiful birthday piГғВұata from a mischievous group of mice. Through teamwork and ingenuity, they learn about friendship while experiencing the joy of a traditional celebration.  0.57 MeiГўВҖВҷs cat Bao refuses to miss out on the Lunar New Year celebrations . As Mei prepares traditional foods with her grandmother, Bao secretly organizes all the neighborhood cats for a spectacular parade that brings the whole community together.  0.76 When strange noises come from Ms. ThompsonГўВҖВҷs brownstone, the neighborhood kids assume itГўВҖВҷs haunted. But Zara and her detective cat Mr. Midnight discover something unexpected about their communityГўВҖВҷs history.  0.78 Deepa discovers an ancient temple in Mumbai filled with magical books containing stories from across India. With her magical tabby cat guide, she must protect the sacred stories from being forgotten.  0.78 Kofi uses his knowledge of West African drum rhythms to solve mysteries in his neighborhood. When instruments start disappearing before the big cultural festival , he must follow the beats to find the truth.  0.81 When Aiden accidentally opens his grandmotherГўВҖВҷs old box, he releases friendly versions of Caribbean folklore creatures. With help from local elders, he learns about his heritage while trying to get the creatures back home.   In the above, youГўВҖВҷll note that none of the summaries have either the word ГўВҖВңfelineГўВҖВқ or ГўВҖВңpartyГўВҖВқ in them. The very first result clearly refers to cats and while it doesnГўВҖВҷt talk about a birthday party, it ranks ahead of the second result, possibly, because the birthday piГғВұata is clearly for a party while the New YearГўВҖВҷs Even celebration might not associate strong enough with a party.  The third and fourth results have ГўВҖВңcatГўВҖВқ, but donГўВҖВҷt have anything to do with ГўВҖВңpartyГўВҖВқ, so their distances is are much farther. The fifth result has no cats (which presumably the embedding knows is the most important factor), but does mention a festival.  The last result mentions neither cats nor parties, but ГўВҖВңpartyГўВҖВқ is presumably closer in the vector space to ГўВҖВңfriendlyГўВҖВқ, so thatГўВҖВҷs why itГўВҖВҷs ranked higher. If you increase the optional distance cutoff, youГўВҖВҷll get more and more results which donГўВҖВҷt appear to obviously match your query.  That last point is a key difference between text search and semantic search: text search doesnГўВҖВҷt return records which donГўВҖВҷt match, but canГўВҖВҷt understand the meaning of your search.  Semantic search understands the meaning, but canГўВҖВҷt really filter out results which donГўВҖВҷt match. These technologies are powerful, but theyГўВҖВҷre still in their infancy.  Larger Data Sets  This is only the beginning. Later, if you want to do more, you might want to dump entire books into a vector database. Beware! You need to break the texts into smaller, overlapping chunks to ensure you donГўВҖВҷt exceed the limit of tokens when the strings are turned into embeddings. They need to overlap to ensure you can get context from chunk to chunk. You probably need to return unique results for that, but decide how to rank them. If one book gets five hits and another only gets two, do you use the number of hits, the match distance, or what?  Conclusion  Vector databases arenГўВҖВҷt magic wands and they donГўВҖВҷt replace regular databases, but the ability to search semantically is a powerful one. Trying to make this work with traditional databases or search engines often involves a lot of work trying to label your data. In fact, theyГўВҖВҷre so powerful that Elastic is now offering vector database searching in ElasticSearch and Lucene  .  So thatГўВҖВҷs it. This is only a toy example and would need a lot more work for production code, but hopefully it will give a rough idea of what you can do with vector databases, and better yet, how to leverage this in Perl!
title: Using Vector Databases with Perl

body: Preface  My Shopping App  Why Not Copilot?  My First Prompt  Challenges  No Editor Integration  Context Length  XCode is Buggy  Mansplaining as a Service  Criticisms  You DonГўВҖВҷt Need ChatGPT  Hallucinations  ГўВҖВңBadГўВҖВқ Code  The Near Future  The Far Future  Conclusion     Preface  IГўВҖВҷve been asked to speak at the 2024 German Perl/Raku Workshop in Frankfurt  and the theme of this yearГўВҖВҷs workshop is Generational AI. IГўВҖВҷve been using Copilot for Vim and doing work with Python and the Llama-2  large language model, so I figured IГўВҖВҷd have no problem contributing, but first I had better brush up on my AI background. The ensuing rabbit hole was a nightmare, but worth it.   [1]  IГўВҖВҷve been working with ChatGPT 4 long enough and seen enough articles about people building full applications with it that I thought it was time to give it a try, but I wanted an app I could use, not some toy for a demo.  My Shopping App  When I go shopping, I use the Notes application on my iPhone. IГўВҖВҷve long hated it. I add items that I want to buy to a list. My list is long and when I search for an item and I donГўВҖВҷt see it, I add it again and I have duplicates. I frequently find IГўВҖВҷve added ГўВҖВңriceГўВҖВқ multiple times.  I wanted a new app, but the App store was a joke. Asking a shopping list application not to track me across the Internet? Having to view ads if I donГўВҖВҷt pay for the privilege of making a shopping list? The opportunity to send my grocery list to my friends and family?  I wanted a dead simple application to handle my grocery list, so I asked ChatGPT to build one for me. It was my first time building an app with ChatGPT and it took me about two hours. Much of that was my learning curve. Getting used to this means I can make these apps even faster.     XCode project of my Shopping App    ItГўВҖВҷs worth noting that while IГўВҖВҷve done Android development with Kotlin, I know nothing about the iOS ecosystem or the Swift programming language. The first time I thought about developing apps for the iPhone was back in the early days, when Objective-C was king and memory management was still manual. I took one look at the code and my wife had to stop me from jabbing a fork in my eye. IГўВҖВҷve never looked back. Until now.  Why Not Copilot?  You might be wondering why I didnГўВҖВҷt just use Copilot? First, Copilot integration with XCode is incredibly painful  and after a long time fighting with it, XCode finally had Copilot, but I couldnГўВҖВҷt get it to make any suggestions, so I just gave up. It wasnГўВҖВҷt the point of what I was trying to do.  Second, CopilotГўВҖВҷs just not a good fit for this (yet). Putting asideГўВҖВ”for the momentГўВҖВ”the problems with hallucinations, you can think of Copilot as autocomplete for methods. Where do I put those methods in an XCode application? I had no clue. ChatGPT, however, is autocomplete for ideas . ItГўВҖВҷs ГўВҖВңtop downГўВҖВқ instead of ГўВҖВңbottom upГўВҖВқ and thatГўВҖВҷs exactly what I needed.   [2]  My First Prompt  Since IГўВҖВҷve been doing a deep dive into this area, I had a pretty good idea of what to expect. The clearer and more structured your prompt is to ChatGPT, the better your output (note: this is all ChatGPT 4. Earlier versions are far less capable). So I approached this like a product owner, detailing the exact requirements for the application that the customer (me) would need.   I would like to create a small shopping list app in Swift for the iPhone. I have a newer version of XCode.   The app should allow me to enter and delete food items to buy.  Each entry should have a checkbox to the left. Unchecked items should appear at the top, checked items at the bottom.   [3]  At the top of the screen should be a large title which says ГўВҖВңShopping.ГўВҖВқ  At the bottom of the screen should be two buttons. One says ГўВҖВңAlphaГўВҖВқ and the other says ГўВҖВңFrequency.ГўВҖВқ   [4]  If I click on the button which says ГўВҖВңAlphaГўВҖВқ, all items should be sorted first by unchecked and the checked status. A secondary, alphabetical sort for each checked and unchecked section.  If I click on the button which says ГўВҖВңFrequency,ГўВҖВқ all items should be sorted first by unchecked and the checked status. A secondary, sort for the frequency with which I first entered or unchecked each item.  The application needs storage to save the number of times IГўВҖВҷve entered or unchecked each item.  After I close and reopen the app, it should show the list in the last state it was observed.  If I delete an item from the list, it should also be deleted from storage.    That might seem like a lot of work, but the up-front effort pays off.  ChatGPT responded quickly with a detailed outline. IГўВҖВҷve reproduced a shorter version below, but rather than overwhelm the reader, IГўВҖВҷve included the full transcript in this Google document  . IГўВҖВҷve turned on commenting on that document, so feel free to add your own.   Creating a shopping list app with the features youГўВҖВҷve described in SwiftUI is a comprehensive project that will give you a good understanding of many SwiftUI concepts and data persistence. HereГўВҖВҷs a high-level overview of how you can implement it:  Step 1: Define Your Data Model  First, you need to create a data model for your shopping items. This model should include properties for the itemГўВҖВҷs name, checked status, and frequency.   import Foundation struct ShoppingItem: Identifiable, Codable { var id = UUID() var name: String var isChecked: Bool var frequency: Int }   (After this, IГўВҖВҷll skip the detail)  Step 2: Create a View Model  Step 3: Build the User Interface  Step 4: Implement Data Persistence  Step 5: Handling State and Sort Logic  Step 6: Testing and Refinement  Conclusion  This outline provides a framework to get started. Each step requires detailed implementation, and youГўВҖВҷll need to write Swift code in the view model and ContentView to handle the specific behaviors like sorting and persistence. Remember to test each part of your app as you develop it. Good luck!   It was a clear, comprehensive outline of an approach I could take and I immediately hit my first bug:   That looks great! The ShoppingListViewModel is giving me the error ГўВҖВңCannot find ГўВҖВҳsaveItems' in scopeГўВҖВқ. How do I fix that?   And ChatGPT dutifully explained how to fix it.  From there, it was a constant flow of me asking it to explain subsequent steps in more detail, prompting it for bug fixes, and gradually watching an iPhone app come to life before my eyes.  Challenges  No Editor Integration  The single biggest problem currently is that ChatGPT has no direct integration with your editor. You need to constantly copy-n-paste the code. For a non-programmer, this could easily be a daunting task, unsure of where and how to do this. So for now, you can try this at home, but if youГўВҖВҷre not a software developer, you might struggle. However, you can always ask ChatGPT for more information on how to handle this. But that brings us to the problem of context length.  Context Length  Early versions of ChatGPT had a ГўВҖВңcontext lengthГўВҖВқ of about 2,000 tokens. The context length is how much of the conversation the language model remembers. These arenГўВҖВҷt just words, theyГўВҖВҷre tokens. Consider the sentence ГўВҖВңI ainГўВҖВҷt done here.ГўВҖВқ Four words, but five tokens:   [5]   ГўВҖВңIГўВҖВқ  " ainГўВҖВҷtГўВҖВқ  " doneГўВҖВқ  " hereГўВҖВқ  ГўВҖВң.ГўВҖВқ   But some LLMs have much longer tokens!  > open up the new ~125k Baichuan2 vocab > find a single token just for "Guided by Xi Jinping's thoughts of socialism with Chinese characteristics in the new era" > Г°ВҹВҳВ¬ > finds another token just for "On our journey to achieve beautiful ideals and big goals" > Г°ВҹВҘВ№ pic.twitter.com/iJzlk0E1zi — Susan Zhang (@suchenzang) September 14, 2023   As a general rule for ChatGPT, you can multiply token length by 75% to get a rough word count, so 2,000 tokens is about 1,500 words it ГўВҖВңremembersГўВҖВқ in the conversation.  For ChatGPT 4, weГўВҖВҷre just over 4,000 tokens in context length. ThatГўВҖВҷs about 3,000 words, or roughly the length of a standard book chapter.
